An async wait: await asyncio.sleep(5) pauses efficiently. During that wait, other requests, WebSocket messages, and database responses can all be handled concurrently.

Synchronous server: request1 -> wait DB request2 waits request3 waits Async event-driven server: request1 waiting DB request2 runs request3 runs request4 runs One thread can manage many waiting operations at once.
documents.s: Real-world example — 1000 users hitting an API

documents.t: Component Role Example Event Loop Manages everything asyncio.run(main()) Coroutine A special async function async def task(): await something() await Pauses a coroutine and yields control await asyncio.sleep(1) Task A scheduled coroutine asyncio.create_task(task())
❌ Old ✅ Modern @asyncio.coroutine / yield from asyncio.sleep(1) async def / await asyncio.sleep(1)

import asyncio async def foo(): print("Foo start") await asyncio.sleep(1) print("Foo end") async def bar(): print("Bar start") await asyncio.sleep(2) print("Bar end") async def main(): await asyncio.gather(foo(), bar()) asyncio.run(main()) The program reacts to sleep-completion and I/O-completion events instead of blocking the whole program — that's what makes it "event-driven."
while True: if something_finished: resume_coroutine()

Write coroutines using async/await The event loop manages execution Coroutines pause on await The event loop resumes them when their event completes Multiple tasks progress concurrently without threads
✅ Use: asyncio.run(), async def, await, asyncio.gather(), asyncio.create_task() ❌ Avoid old styles: yield from, @asyncio.coroutine

asyncio.gather() accepts both tasks and plain coroutines. asyncio.gather(foo(), bar()) Internally, it converts the coroutines into Tasks automatically — conceptually equivalent to: task1 = asyncio.create_task(foo()) task2 = asyncio.create_task(bar()) await asyncio.gather(task1, task2)

Direct coroutines — await asyncio.gather(foo(), bar()) ✅ Cleaner, most common. Explicit tasks — create tasks first, then gather them. ✅ Needed when you want task references, e.g. to: cancel later check status await later store the tasks

A coroutine runs only when one of these happens: 1. It is directly awaited await foo() info The event loop runs it directly. ❗ It is NOT converted into a Task.And there is no schedule. (Common misconception — this is important.) There is no task creation, zero scheduling, zero queue placement, and zero loop interaction when await child() is called. It is a direct, immediate, inline execution. The Event Loop manages independent Tasks. It does not manage the internal sub-coroutine calls inside an active Task. Explanation: when you do await foo(), the event loop doesn't create a separate Task object to track foo() — instead, the current Task just keeps running foo()'s code as part of itself. No new scheduling entry is created.And waiting and resuming is considering as in the same Task. 2. It's wrapped in a Task asyncio.create_task(foo()) Now the coroutine becomes a Task. 3. It's passed to gather() await asyncio.gather(foo(), bar()) gather() converts coroutines → Tasks automatically. 4. It's passed to asyncio.run() asyncio.run(work()) Internally, Python roughly does: loop = asyncio.new_event_loop() # creating event loop coroutine = work() # creating coroutine object task = loop.create_task(coroutine) # wraps coroutine into a Task # Now the event loop can manage it, because it only schedules Tasks/Futures loop.run_until_complete(task) # loop starts running Key point: await foo() does not create a Task — this is the central misunderstanding many people have.
documents.s: 7. When Does a Coroutine Actually Run?

documents.t: When you write: asyncio.run(main()) Python: Creates an event loop Starts running main() Keeps switching between coroutines whenever they await Stops when everything finishes Normal synchronous code: a(); b(); c() — the CPU waits for each function to fully complete. Async code: await a(); await b(); await c() — if a() is waiting on I/O (network, database, sleep, websocket, file), the event loop pauses it and runs something else. That's the entire async model. Component Purpose Ready Queue Tasks ready to execute now Waiting Tasks Tasks waiting for I/O/timers Selector/Poller OS mechanism to detect events Task Objects Wrap coroutines Callbacks Functions stored now, run later when an event happens Example — timer callback: loop.call_later(5, hello) Meaning: "After 5 seconds, run hello()." The callback is stored now but executed later.
documents.s: 8. Event Loop Internals — The Big Picture

documents.t: async def foo(): await asyncio.sleep(1) foo() # does NOT run it — only creates a coroutine object await foo() → the event loop runs it directly, with no scheduling like Tasks/Futures. The current coroutine pauses and directly runs foo() until foo() itself hits an await. task = asyncio.create_task(foo()) → now the event loop manages it as a Task that wraps the coroutine. The loop only schedules Tasks/Futures, never raw coroutine functions.
documents.s: Coroutine vs Task — calling a coroutine doesn't run it

documents.t: import asyncio async def work(): print("Start") await asyncio.sleep(2) print("End") asyncio.run(work()) Create event loop — loop = EventLoop() Convert coroutine into a Task — task = Task(work()) Put task into the ready queue — ready_queue = [task]
while not stopped: run_ready_tasks() # from ready queue wait_for_io_events() move_completed_io_tasks_to_ready() This runs thousands of times per second.

The event loop picks a task from the queue and calls task._step(), which does both: Starts coroutine execution the first time, or Resumes coroutine execution later (after an await) Your coroutine runs until it finishes or hits an await.
await asyncio.sleep(2) The coroutine effectively says: "I cannot continue now. Resume me later." The task pauses: Task state: RUNNING -> WAITING The event loop removes it from the ready queue.
asyncio.sleep(2) creates a timer. The loop stores resume this task after 2 seconds in an internal timer heap.

If the ready queue is empty, the event loop calls the OS selector: OS Mechanism Linux epoll Mac kqueue Windows IOCP The loop effectively asks the OS: "Wake me when a socket is ready, a timer expires, or data arrives."

After 2 seconds, the OS/timer wakes the event loop, which moves the task back into the ready queue. The loop calls task._step() again, the coroutine continues after the await (print("End")), and then finishes.
Time → ──────────────────────────────── Task starts | print("Start") | await sleep(2) V Task paused Event loop runs other tasks or sleeps 2 sec later V Task resumed | print("End") Task finished

Busy-waiting / spinning means the CPU continuously executes checking code, even when nothing has happened: while True: check_if_done() This can run millions of times per second, repeatedly checking a variable — extremely inefficient. The CPU never truly idles. Asyncio does NOT do this. When nothing is ready (no socket data, no expired timers, no ready tasks), the event loop tells the OS: "Put me to sleep until something happens," roughly via: selector.select(timeout=5) This blocks efficiently — the Python thread sleeps inside an OS syscall, and the CPU is free for other work. No repeated Python-level checking happens. So: event loop sleeping ≠ CPU fully busy. CPU usage stays very low while waiting.
documents.s: 10. CPU Usage: Why Async Is Efficient (Busy-Waiting vs Sleeping)

documents.t: An OS/hardware interrupt wakes the sleeping selector. Then selector.select() returns, the event loop runs again, and the task resumes. Correct statement: the OS selector/wait system, triggered by a hardware/OS event, wakes the sleeping event loop thread.

response = await session.get(url) Internally: The socket is registered with the OS selector The task pauses The loop runs other tasks The OS reports the socket has data The loop resumes the task This is the heart of async networking — the event loop hands the "waiting" job to the OS selector; when data arrives, the OS informs the selector, and meanwhile the event loop may be running other tasks (or sleeping, if the ready queue is empty).

Ready Queue — deque([task1, task2]), tasks ready immediately Scheduled Timers — usually a min-heap ordered by time: [(resume_at, task)] Selector — tracks sockets/file descriptors: socket -> task waiting
Asyncio's event loop waits for two kinds of events: Type Example I/O events socket data arrived Timer events sleep timeout expired
documents.s: 12. The Two Waiting Types: I/O vs Timers

documents.t: await socket.recv() The task waits for socket readiness; the OS selector tracks the file descriptor. Wakeup chain: hardware/network interrupt → OS marks socket ready → selector wakes. NOte- A file descriptor (FD) is just a number (an integer) that the operating system uses as a handle to refer to an open "thing" — could be a file, a socket, a pipe, a device, etc.

await asyncio.sleep(5) This is not waiting for network I/O. Instead, asyncio stores (resume_at_time, task) — e.g. (10:30:05, task1) — usually inside a min-heap, so the smallest wakeup time stays on top. ("Timer heap" and "min-heap" refer to the same structure here.) This tells the event loop: *"When is the next timer due?"

If the next timer in the timer heap expires in 5 seconds, the event loop does: selector.select(timeout=5) meaning: "Sleep until an I/O event occurs OR the 5-second timeout expires" — whichever comes first. The single OS waiting syscall (linux -> .select()) handles both I/O waiting and timer-timeout waiting together. Example: async def task_sleep(): await asyncio.sleep(5) # Task X — pure timer, no I/O async def task_network(): await socket.recv() # Task Y — pure I/O, no timer Task X (asyncio.sleep(5)) — this task itself is not registered with the OS selector at all. It doesn't touch any file descriptor. It only adds an entry to asyncio's own internal timer heap: (wake_at=T+5, Task X). The selector doesn't know Task X exists. Task Y (socket.recv()) — this task is registered with the OS selector. Its socket's file descriptor gets registered: "tell me when FD 7 is readable." The timer heap doesn't know Task Y exists. Now — the event loop has to wait for either of these to become ready. It only has one mechanism for blocking efficiently: the OS selector call. So it does this: Looks at the timer heap → finds the soonest timer → Task X needs 5 seconds. Calls selector.select(timeout=5) — registered FDs include Task Y's socket (FD 7). The timeout=5 value comes from Task X's timer, purely so the call doesn't block forever if Task Y's socket never becomes ready. So this one syscall is simultaneously: watching Task Y's socket (real I/O registration) bounded by Task X's timer (just a numeric timeout, no FD involved) If Task Y's socket gets data after 2 seconds → select() returns early at 2s, with Task Y's FD in the ready list. Task X's 5-second timer hasn't expired — Task X stays in the timer heap, untouched. If nothing arrives on Task Y's socket for the full 5 seconds → select() returns at 5s with an empty I/O list. The event loop checks the timer heap, sees Task X's timer expired, moves Task X to ready.Task Y is not expired, not cancelled, not finished, not touched at all. It's still just sitting there, waiting, exactly as before. Summary * asyncio.sleep(5) is not waiting for I/O — true, for that specific task. It never touches a file descriptor or the selector's registration list. * selector.select() handles I/O and timeouts together — also true, but at the event loop level, across all tasks combined. The selector call's timeout parameter is just borrowed from the timer heap's soonest entry — it doesn't mean the sleep itself becomes an I/O operation.
documents.s: Both waits happen together at syscall(selctor)

documents.t: If no socket event occurs, after 5 seconds the OS timeout expires, selector.select() returns, the event loop checks the timer heap ("timer expired"), moves the task to the ready queue, and resumes the coroutine. Bottom line: the timer heap is just asyncio's internal structure for knowing how long selector.select(timeout=...) should sleep. The actual wakeup always comes through the OS waiting system — triggered either by I/O readiness or by timeout expiration. selector.select() always returns a list of ready events - here if no socket event happens , it will return [].
CREATED ↓ READY ↓ RUNNING ↓ WAITING (await) ↓ READY ↓ FINISHED
await means: "Suspend THIS coroutine and let the event loop run others." Without any await (e.g. while True: pass), the event loop freezes, because the coroutine never yields control.
Asyncio uses cooperative multitasking — tasks voluntarily pause using await. Unlike threads: No OS thread switching No true parallel execution (usually) One thread runs many coroutines
1 thread 1 event loop many coroutines Only one coroutine executes at any instant. Switching only happens at await points.

while True: # run ready tasks for task in ready_queue: task.run_until_next_await() # check timers move_expired_timers_to_ready() # wait for socket events events = selector.select() # wake waiting tasks for event in events: ready_queue.append(event.task)
await foo() runs sequentially. But asyncio.create_task(foo()) tells the loop to schedule it independently in the background, so multiple tasks can progress together.

A Future is a low-level placeholder object representing "a value/result that will be available later." Think of it as an empty box now — the value comes later. A Future internally stores: pending/done state result exception callbacks future = asyncio.Future() # Future: PENDING future.set_result(100) # Future: DONE, Result: 100

Suppose a coroutine does: await socket.recv() The socket data isn't ready immediately, so asyncio creates a Future representing "future socket data." The task pauses, waiting for that Future. When the data arrives: OS wakes selector → asyncio gets data → future.set_result(data) Now the waiting task resumes. Not everything is a coroutine — sockets, callbacks, and external events also need a generic way to represent "something finishes later." Futures unify all of these.

result = await future If the result isn't ready, the current task pauses and the event loop runs others. When the future gets its result, the task resumes and await returns the value. Who actually uses Futures directly? Mostly low-level asyncio internals: socket operations, transport/protocol APIs, event loop callbacks, and bridging callback-style code into async code.

Task IS-A Future isinstance(task, asyncio.Future) # True Future ↑ Task Task inherits Future's behavior (result, done state, exception, callbacks) plus its own(task's) coroutine execution and scheduling. Future Task What it is Just a container for a future result Actively executes a coroutine inside the event loop Runs code? No Yes

Future, completed manually: import asyncio async def main(): future = asyncio.Future() future.set_result("hello") result = await future print(result) asyncio.run(main()) # Output: hello The Future itself didn't execute anything — you completed it manually. Task, runs a coroutine automatically: import asyncio async def work(): await asyncio.sleep(1) return 42 async def main(): task = asyncio.create_task(work()) result = await task print(result) asyncio.run(main()) The Task automatically runs the coroutine and eventually stores the result internally, just like a Future.

task = asyncio.create_task(work()) Roughly: Task contains: coroutine object, state, result, callbacks The event loop repeatedly calls task._step() until the coroutine finishes. Then the task internally does future.set_result(return_value) — which is exactly why a Task behaves like a Future. Because Task inherits Future, await task means "wait until this task's underlying future becomes DONE." Defines/does Coroutine Defines async work Future Placeholder for a result Task Runs the coroutine and stores the result One-line definitions: Future — "I will eventually contain a result." Task — "I am executing a coroutine and will eventually contain its result."
await asyncio.gather(a(), b()) Internally: coroutines → Tasks, and those Tasks are tracked via Futures.

EVENT LOOP ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Ready Queue │ │ ┌─────┐ │ │ │Task1│ │ │ │Task2│ │ │ └─────┘ │ │ │ │ Timers Heap │ │ sleep tasks │ │ │ │ Selector │ │ sockets/files │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────┘ Key mental model: while True: run_some_task() pause_tasks_waiting_for_io() resume_tasks_when_io_ready()
uvloop is a faster replacement for Python's default asyncio event loop. It's built on top of libuv — the same high-performance library used internally by Node.js.

# Default import asyncio asyncio.run(main()) # uses asyncio's built-in DefaultEventLoop # With uvloop import asyncio import uvloop asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy()) asyncio.run(main()) Your async code stays mostly the same — only the engine underneath changes. Shortcut form: import asyncio import uvloop uvloop.install() asyncio.run(main()) (uvloop.install() is shorthand for setting the event loop policy.)

The default asyncio loop is implemented mostly in Python. uvloop is implemented in C, using libuv and optimized system calls — so socket handling, scheduling, callbacks, timers, and polling all become much faster. Architecture comparison: Normal asyncio: uvloop: Python asyncio loop Python asyncio APIs ↓ ↓ selectors uvloop (epoll/kqueue) ↓ ↓ libuv (C library) OS ↓ OS libuv handles epoll (Linux), kqueue (Mac), IOCP (Windows), timers, async sockets, a thread pool, and file I/O very efficiently in C. Result: lower latency, higher throughput, less overhead — especially for web servers, websockets, APIs, and high-concurrency networking. Frameworks like FastAPI, aiohttp, and Uvicorn commonly recommend it. pip install uvloop

uvloop is not a new async syntax, a different coroutine system, or a framework — it only replaces the event loop engine underneath asyncio. Analogy: your async code is the driver, the event loop is the car engine. uvloop gives you a faster engine — same driver, same road, better performance. Limitation: uvloop mainly improves I/O-bound async applications. It does not speed up CPU-heavy calculations, blocking synchronous code, or bad async patterns. Default asyncio uvloop Official Python Yes No Built into Python Yes No Modern Yes Yes Speed Good Usually faster Uses C/libuv No Yes Best for beginners Yes Not needed initially Production performance Good Excellent
These patterns are common ways to structure async programs to manage concurrency, scheduling, cancellation, coordination, background jobs, and producer/consumer systems.
async def main(): await task1() await task2() await task3() Execution: task1 finishes, then task2, then task3 — no concurrency. Use this when each task depends on the previous result.

async def main(): t1 = asyncio.create_task(task1()) t2 = asyncio.create_task(task2()) await t1 await t2 Both tasks run at the same time. await t1 means "pause until task t1 finishes" — not "start the task." The task already started earlier, the moment create_task() ran: Coroutine object created Task object created Task added to the event loop's ready queue (ready_queue.append(task)) Whenever the event loop next gets control (at any await point), it begins executing it.

The most common concurrency pattern: results = await asyncio.gather( task1(), task2(), task3() ) Runs all concurrently and returns results in order. If you pass coroutines, they're automatically converted into Tasks — you can pass tasks, coroutines, or futures into gather(). Equivalent (but more verbose) to show the order: t1 = create_task(task1()) t2 = create_task(task2()) await t1 await t2 Use this when you need all the results together.

asyncio.create_task(background_worker()) No await — the task runs independently. async def logger(): while True: await asyncio.sleep(5) print("logging") asyncio.create_task(logger()) ⚠️ Danger: asyncio.run() keeps the event loop alive only until the main coroutine completes — not until every background task finishes. When the loop shuts down, remaining tasks get cancelled. So asyncio.create_task(...) does not guarantee task completion — if nobody awaits it and the program exits, the task may die unfinished.

Uses asyncio.Queue(): import asyncio queue = asyncio.Queue() async def producer(): for i in range(5): await queue.put(i) async def consumer(): while True: item = await queue.get() print(item) queue.task_done() Flow: Producer → Queue → Consumer A queue is a temporary storage structure for passing data between producers and consumers, following FIFO order (First In → First Out). Real uses: message systems, websocket processing, job workers, API pipelines. Walking through get()/put(): await queue.put(10) await queue.put(20) # Queue: [10, 20] item = await queue.get() # -> 10 # Queue: [20] # next get() -> 20 Why await? Because the queue may be empty when get() is called — the consumer pauses until the producer adds something. Likewise, queue.put() is awaitable because the queue might be full. By default, asyncio.Queue() is unbounded, so await queue.put(x) returns immediately. But you can bound it: queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=2) await queue.put(1) await queue.put(2) # Queue: [1, 2] — now full await queue.put(3) # cannot insert immediately — producer pauses The producer task becomes WAITING until a consumer removes an item via await queue.get(), freeing up space; then the producer wakes and continues.

Multiple workers consume the same queue: workers = [ asyncio.create_task(worker()) for _ in range(5) ] Now 5 workers process jobs concurrently. Used in crawlers, API fetchers, and task-processing systems. A crawler is a program that automatically visits many web pages and collects information — e.g. starting at https://example.com, it downloads the page, extracts links, visits those links, and repeats.

Without limits, await asyncio.gather(*many_requests) could fire off 10,000 requests at once — a bad idea. Use a semaphore instead: sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10) async def fetch(): async with sem: await api_call() Only 10 concurrent tasks are allowed at a time. Used for rate limits, DB connections, and API throttling.
Prevent infinite waiting: await asyncio.wait_for(task(), timeout=5) If the task exceeds the timeout, it's cancelled and a TimeoutError is raised.
task.cancel() Inside the coroutine: try: await something() except asyncio.CancelledError: cleanup() raise Cancellation is cooperative — the task stops at the next await.

asyncio.Event is used for signaling between coroutines. One coroutine says "wait until something happens," another says "okay, it happened now." event = asyncio.Event() # initially NOT SET await event.wait() # pauses if not set event.set() # wakes all waiters event.clear() # resets to NOT SET Use cases: coordination, readiness notification, startup synchronization. import asyncio event = asyncio.Event() async def waiter(): print("Waiting for signal...") await event.wait() print("Signal received!") async def sender(): await asyncio.sleep(2) print("Sending signal") event.set() async def main(): await asyncio.gather(waiter(), sender()) asyncio.run(main()) Output: Waiting for signal... (after 2 sec) Sending signal Signal received! Internal flow: event starts False. waiter() calls await event.wait(); since it's false, the waiter task pauses. After 2 seconds, sender() calls event.set(), making it True — the event loop wakes all tasks waiting on that event. Note that event.set() does not block; it simply signals "you may continue now." Multiple coroutines can wait on the same event, and all of them wake up together when it's set.

Prevent race conditions: lock = asyncio.Lock() async with lock: shared_data += 1 Only one coroutine enters the critical section at a time. This is needed because coroutines can switch at any await point. What is a race condition? It happens when multiple tasks access/modify shared data at the same time, and the final result depends on execution order — causing wrong values, lost updates, or inconsistent state. Example: suppose counter = 0 and two coroutines both do counter += 1. You'd expect 2, but you might get 1. That's a race condition. Why it happens: counter += 1 is not one atomic operation — internally it's roughly: Read counter Calculate new value Write counter If a task switch (due to an await) happens in the middle, before the write, bad things happen.

Modern structured concurrency: async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg: tg.create_task(task1()) tg.create_task(task2()) Safer than raw create_task. Benefit: if one task fails, all related tasks are cancelled automatically, with better cleanup. Python 3.11+ prefers TaskGroup over unmanaged background tasks.
Chain async stages: Fetch → Parse → Save. Each stage is async, often connected via queues.

These names come from flow-diagram visuals — how work spreads out and then comes back together. Fan-out — one thing splits into many tasks, like a fan opening outward: Task1 / Main ──┼── Task2 \ Task3 await asyncio.gather( fetch(url1), fetch(url2), fetch(url3) ) One main flow spreads into many concurrent tasks — that's fan-out. Fan-in — after many tasks finish, results come back into one place: Task1 ─\ Task2 ──┼── Final Result Task3 ─/ results = await asyncio.gather( fetch(url1), fetch(url2), fetch(url3) ) All results combine into results — that combining-back step is fan-in. Used in parallel API calls, distributed requests, and crawlers.

for _ in range(3): try: return await api_call() except Exception: await asyncio.sleep(1) Common in networking. Walking through it: Attempt 1: api_call() fails (e.g. ConnectionError, TimeoutError) → jumps to except Exception: → await asyncio.sleep(1) waits 1 second → loop continues. Attempt 2: tries again. If it fails again, same except/sleep cycle. Attempt 3: same process. On any success: return await api_call() returns the value immediately — the loop exits, no more retries. Visual: Attempt 1 → failed ↓ wait 1 sec Attempt 2 → failed ↓ wait 1 sec Attempt 3 → success ↓ return result
Prevent overload using a bounded queue: queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=100) The producer pauses if the queue is full. Very important in real systems.
Protect a task from outer cancellation: await asyncio.shield(task) An outer cancellation won't stop (cancel) the protected task.
Producer ↓ Queue ↓ Worker Pool ↓ Semaphore-limited HTTP requests ↓ Gather results
documents.s: Putting it together — a common real architecture

documents.t: await create_task gather Queue producer-consumer Semaphore Cancellation TaskGroup

Problem without a connection pool: if 100 users hit your site and every request creates a new DB connection — connect to postgres run query disconnect — this is expensive, because creating a DB connection involves a TCP handshake, authentication, memory allocation, and DB process/thread setup. Doing this thousands of times repeatedly is slow. Solution: create a handful of connections once and reuse them: conn = await create_db_connection() # Pool: conn1, conn2, conn3 Tasks borrow one (conn = await pool.get()) and return it when done (await pool.put(conn)). Purpose: avoid expensive connection creation, limit max connections, improve performance. Real libraries already do this internally — Django's DB connections, SQLAlchemy's pool, asyncpg's pool, aiomysql's pool.

import asyncio class Connection: def __init__(self, id): self.id = id async def query(self): await asyncio.sleep(1) print(f"Query using connection {self.id}") class AsyncConnectionPool: def __init__(self, size): self.pool = asyncio.Queue() for i in range(size): conn = Connection(i) self.pool.put_nowait(conn) async def acquire(self): print("Waiting for connection...") conn = await self.pool.get() print(f"Acquired connection {conn.id}") return conn async def release(self, conn): print(f"Released connection {conn.id}") await self.pool.put(conn) async def worker(name, pool): conn = await pool.acquire() try: print(f"{name} started work") await conn.query() print(f"{name} finished work") finally: await pool.release(conn) async def main(): pool = AsyncConnectionPool(size=2) await asyncio.gather( worker("A", pool), worker("B", pool), worker("C", pool), worker("D", pool), ) asyncio.run(main()) Here 4 workers using 2 connections from connection pool

Initially: Queue = [conn0, conn1] A starts → conn = await pool.acquire() → gets conn0 B starts → gets conn1 C starts → queue empty → await self.pool.get() suspends C; it waits asynchronously A finishes → await pool.release(conn0) puts the connection back → C wakes up and gets it

Why a Queue for the pool? Thread-safe/async-safe coordination Automatic waiting FIFO ordering Why await pool.get()? If no connection exists, the coroutine pauses, the event loop runs other tasks, and there's no CPU blocking. Why finally? Very important — even if query() crashes: finally: await pool.release(conn) ensures the connection returns to the pool. Otherwise, a connection leak can happen. Note on put_nowait(): self.pool.put_nowait(conn) means "put this item into the queue immediately without waiting." In a connection pool: the connections/resources are the pool, and the queue stores the available connections. Pool of connections: -------------- conn1 conn2 conn3 -------------- Usually implemented internally using asyncio.Queue(). So here: Pool = the reusable connections (conn1, conn2, conn3 — the actual resources). Queue = the data structure managing which connections are currently available, e.g. self.pool = asyncio.Queue() containing [conn1, conn2, conn3].

A worker pool manages workers doing jobs/tasks — e.g. 3 workers processing 100 jobs. Workers continuously pull from a queue (job = await queue.get()) and process it. Purpose: limit concurrency, distribute workload. Structurally, a connection pool looks very similar to a worker pool, because both use a shared queue/resource, waiting tasks, and limited concurrency. But the meaning is different. In a worker pool, workers are permanent/background consumers: jobs = asyncio.Queue() # each worker: while True: job = await jobs.get() await process(job) Flow: Job Queue → Workers process jobs. The workers themselves are the pool. So: the workers are the pool, and the jobs/tasks are stored in the queue. Job Queue ------------- job1 job2 job3 ------------- ↓ ↓ Worker1 Worker2 Worker3

import asyncio queue = asyncio.Queue() async def worker(name): while True: job = await queue.get() print(f"{name} processing {job}") await asyncio.sleep(2) print(f"{name} finished {job}") queue.task_done() async def main(): workers = [ asyncio.create_task(worker("W1")), asyncio.create_task(worker("W2")), ] for i in range(5): await queue.put(f"job-{i}") await queue.join() for w in workers: w.cancel() asyncio.run(main()) Here 2 workers processing 5 jobs in the worker pool.
Connection Pool: Task A → conn1 Task B → conn2 Task C waits Worker Pool: Worker1 ← job1 Worker2 ← job2 job3 waits
Queue = Pool = Worker Pool work waiting workers Connection Pool available connections connections themselves

Pending/Available The "pool" itself Worker Pool Pending jobs/tasks Workers Connection Pool Available connections Reusable connections/resources Worker Pool Connection Pool What's pooled Task executors Reusable resources Example Background workers DB connections Relationship Workers consume jobs Tasks borrow resources Concern Work management Resource management Both commonly use asyncio.Queue, semaphores, and the producer-consumer pattern — which is why they feel conceptually similar.
A queue is not always a pool. They are different concepts.
documents.s: 20. Queue vs Pool — They Are NOT the Same Thing

documents.t: A queue is just a data structure. It stores things in order. It can hold jobs, messages, connections, events — anything. Purpose: temporary waiting/storage. queue = asyncio.Queue()
A pool is a group of reusable, limited resources — e.g. a worker pool, connection pool, or thread pool. Purpose: reuse limited resources efficiently.
It's very common for a pool to be implemented internally using a queue, because a queue helps manage waiting, coordination, and availability. But the queue itself is not the pool.
queue = asyncio.Queue() await queue.put("job1") await queue.put("job2") This is just a queue of jobs. No pool exists here.
documents.s: Example 1 — a plain queue (NOT a pool)

documents.t: pool = asyncio.Queue() pool.put_nowait(conn1) pool.put_nowait(conn2) Here, the connections are the pool; the queue manages which connections are currently available.

put_nowait() await put() Timing Immediate Async operation Can suspend? No Yes, if the queue is full Typical use During setup, when you know the queue isn't full Inside async runtime flow Suspend means the function pauses execution and gives control back to the event loop, without blocking the whole program.
documents.s: Key difference: put_nowait() vs await put()

documents.t: workers = [ create_task(worker()), create_task(worker()), ] Here, the workers are the pool; the queue (the queue is used inside the worker's logic (await queue.get() appears inside worker()), not that the queue object is created/scoped inside the function( worker()).) stores the jobs.
Queue answers: "What is waiting?" Pool answers: "What reusable, limited resources exist?"

Exception handling allows a program to handle unexpected errors during execution in a controlled way,instead of crashing it abruptly. eg: Handles runtime errors such as invalid input, file not found, type mismatches,division by zero that occur during runtime.(run and execution are same meaning in many languages) Compile time errors found by compiler/interpreter before program starts running eg : (Syntax,Missing parenthesis).So Exception handling deals with only runtime erros. try: #code except: #code else: #code finally: #code try : Runs the risky code that might cause an error. except : Catches and handles errors. else : excecute only if no exception occurs in try. finally : Runs regardless of what happens useful for cleanup tasks like closing files. example try: n=0 res=100/n except ZeroDivisionError: print("You can't divide by 0") else: print("result",res) finally: print("Excecution completes")

ZeroDivisionError: Raised when dividing a number by zero. ValueError: Raised when a function receives an invalid value. TypeError: Raised when an operation is performed on incompatible data types. FileNotFoundError: Raised when a file does not exist. ImportError: Raised when Python cannot import a module.
We can handle errors more efficeintly by specifying the types of exceptions we expect.This will make code more safe and debug easily

try: # This will cause ValueError x = int("str") inv = 1 / x # Inverse calculation except ValueError: print("Not Valid!") except ZeroDivisionError: print("Zero has no inverse!") "str" can't converted into int. But "2" can convert into int.

We can catch multiple exceptions in single block if we needs to handle them same way or we can separate them if different types of exceptions needs different handling. a=["10","twenty",30] try: total=int(a[0]) + int(a[1]) except (ValueError,TypeError) as e: print("Error",e) except IndexError: print("index out of range")

Catch-all handler is used to catch any exception.Use only except keyword to define it. try: # Risky operation: dividing string by number res = "100" / 20 except ArithmeticError: print("Arithmetic problem.") except: print("Something went wrong!") Here type error happened, so catches at except .

We rasie exception using raise keyword followed by instance of the exception class that we wants to trigger. syntax rasie ExceptionType("Error message") example def set(age): if age < 0: raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative.") print(f"Age set to {age}") try: set(-5) except ValueError as e: print(e)

Error and Exceptions are both issues in the program, but they differ in severity and handling. Errors : Issues in the program that occurs at compile time eg:SyntaxError,IndentationError or during execution. Exception : Problems that occur at runtime and can be managed using exception handling.eg : Invalid input, missing files. Bugs : A mistake in the code ,design or logic made by programmer.

Python allows one try-except block to be placed inside another try-except block. def divide_and_access(a, b): try: result = a // b print("Result:", result) try: nums = [1, 2, 3] print(nums[5]) except IndexError: print("Invalid index access") except ZeroDivisionError: print("Cannot divide by zero") finally: print("Execution completed") divide_and_access(6, 2) print("---") divide_and_access(6, 0)

Cannot divide by zero Execution completed ` Explanation The inner try-except block handles the IndexError caused by accessing an invalid list index. The outer try-except block handles the ZeroDivisionError when dividing by zero. The finally block runs in both cases.
documents.s:
Output
`
Result: 3
Invalid index access
Execution completed

documents.t: Python provides a set of built-in exceptions, each designed to a specific type of error and helps to debug more effectively.

This class is the root of python's exception hierarchy.All other exceptions directly or indirectly inherited from it.But this is rarely used in the code, it is important because it is the foundations of Python's error-handling system.

Exception class is the base for all the non-exist exceptions.You will often catch Exception in general error-handling when you are not target to specific exception. try: raise Exception("This is a generic exception") except Exception as e: print(e) Explanation: We manually raise an Exception with a message. The error is caught and message is displayed instead of halting the program.

ArithmeticError class is the base for all errors related to mathematical operations. You don't raise it directly, but provides a way to catch all math-related errors in one block. try: raise ArithmeticError("Arithmetic error occured") except ArithmeticError as e: print(e) Explanation: We raise an ArithmeticError with a custom message. The program catches it and prints the error message, preventing a crash.

It occurs when you try to divide a number by zero. try: result = 10 / 0 except ZeroDivisionError as e: print(e) Explanation: operation 10/0 is invalid. Python raises a ZeroDivisionError, which is then caught in the except block. Instead of crashing, program prints the error message.

OverflowError occurs when the result of numerical operations is too large for Python to represent.While it handles large integers well,certain floating point operations (like very large exponentials) can still cause error. import math try: result = math.exp(1000) # Exponential function with a large argument except OverflowError as e: print(e) Explanation: exponential function with input 1000 produces a number too large to handle. Python raises an OverflowError, which is caught and displayed as an error message.

Main FloatingPoint conditions are Condition Meaning Example Divide by zero Finite number divided by 0 1.0 / 0.0 Overflow Result too large to represent 1e308 * 1e308 Underflow Result too small to represent accurately 1e-308 * 1e-308 Invalid operation No meaningful numeric result 0.0 / 0.0, sqrt(-1) (real arithmetic) FloatingPointErrors occurs when a floating-point operation("floating-point calculation" ≈ "operation on floating-point numbers") encounters a numerical condition such as divide-by-zero, overflow,underflow or invalid arithmetic. By default Python handles all floating-point issues silently(Dividing by zero results in inf or nan). However we can enable the error explicitly using the libraries like NumPY. Example: This example reports error in NumPY and performs a division by zero to trigger FloatingPointError. import numpy as np np.seterr(all='raise') try: np.divide(1.0,0) output FloatingPointError caught: divide by zero encountered in divide Explanation: Normally, NumPy would return inf for 1/0. But since we enabled error raising, the division explicitly throws a FloatingPointError, which we catch and print. info np.divide(1/0) here the inputs are integers, but the NumPy processes the operation using floating-point-aware numerical machinery and detects a floating-point exceptioon condition.The FloatingPointError occurs normally on floating point values. Without np.seterr(all=raise),NumPY usually return inf or nan ,emits warning and continues execution. RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in divide. With np.seterr(all=raise) now NumPY converts floating-point condition into exception.FloatingPointError: divide by zero encountered in divide

AssertionError is raised when aseertion statment fails. The assert keyword is often used for debugging or testing assumptions in code. Example: This example checks if 1===2 using assert.Since condition is false , it raises an AssertionError try: assert 1==2 , "Assertion failed" except AssertionError as e: print(e) output Assertion failed Explanation: assert statement expects 1 == 2 to be true, but it isn’t. Therefore, Python raises an AssertionError with the message "Assertion failed".

AttributeError happens when try to access an attribute that doesn't exist for an object. Example: This example tries to access a non-existent attribute in a class instance. class MyClass: pass obj=Myclass() try: obj.some_attribute except AttributeError as e: print(e) output 'MyClass' object has no attribute 'some_attribute' Explanation: object obj is created from MyClass, which has no attributes. Attempting to access some_attribute raises an AttributeError.

IndexError happens when tries to access a list element(sequence) with an index of out of range. Example: This example tries to access the 6th element of a list that only has 3 elements. my_list=[1,2,3] try: my_list[5] except IndexError as e: print(e) output list index out of range Explanation: list has only indices 0, 1, 2. When we try to access index 5, Python raises an IndexError.

KeyError happens when you tries access a key that is doesn't exist. Example: This example tries to access the key "key2" in a dictionary that only has "key1". d = {"key1": "value1"} try: val = d["key2"] except KeyError as e: print(e)

MemoryError happens when python cannot allocate enough memory for an opertaion. This usually happens when tries to create extremely large data structures. try: li = [1] * (10**10) except MemoryError as e: print(e) Explanation: Attempting to create a list with 10**10 elements requires more memory than available. Python raises a MemoryError.
NameError occurs when you use a variable of functio name that doesnot defined. try: print(var) except NameError as e: print(e) output name 'var' is not defined.

Raised when system related opertion(like file i/o,opening files,interacting with os) fails. IOError is just an alias for OSError(They are the same) FIleNotFoundError is a subclass of OSError, specificallly raised when a file or directory doesn't exist. Example: This example attempts to open a missing file, which triggers FileNotFoundError (a subclass of OSError). try: open("non_existent_file.txt") # File does not exist except FileNotFoundError as e: # More specific print("FileNotFoundError caught:", e) except OSError as e: # General OS-related error print("OSError caught:", e) output FileNotFoundError caught: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'non_existent_file.txt' Explanation: missing file raises FileNotFoundError, which is a subclass of OSError. Since we catch it specifically, the error is displayed clearly.

Exception Name Description TypeError Raised when an operation or function applied to object of inappropriate type(eg:"fathima"+4 ) ValueError Raised when a function receives an argument of correct type but with an invalid value(eg : int("fathima") it should be int("5") ) ImportError Raised when there is a problem with an import statement ModuleNotFoundError Raised when a module cann't found StopIteration Raised when next() function is called , but the iterator has no more items. KeyboardInterrupt Raised when the user interrupts the program's execution(eg:pressing Ctrl+C) SystemExit Raised when sys.exit() is called to terminate the program NotImplementedError Raised when a method that should be implemented a subclass but not implemented. RuntimeError that is raised when something goes wrong during program execution, but the error doesn't fit a more specific exception type, so when the error doesn't fall under other categories and fails at execution time RecursionError Raised when maximum recursion depth is exceeded. SyntaxError Raised when there is a mistake in the python syntax IndentationError Raised when the indentation is not in Python code TabError Raised when there is an inconsistent use of tabs and spaces in indentation. UnicodeError Raised when Encoding or Decoding Unicode text fails.

User-defined exceptions are created by defining new class that inherits from Python builtin Exception class or any of its subclass. Steps to create and use Userdefined exception class Define a new Exception class - Create a new class that inherits from Exception class or any of its subclass. Raise the Exception - Use the raise statement to raise the user defined exception when a specific condition occurs, Handle Excption - Use the try-except blocks to handle the user defined exception. example #step-1 - Define a custom exception class class InvalidAgeError(Exception): def __init__(self,age,message="Age must be between 0 and 120"): self.age=age self.message=message super().__init__(self.message) def __str__(self): return f"{self.age} -> {self.msg}" #step-2 - Use the custome excption in your code def set_age(age): if age<0 or age age >120: raise InvalidAgError(age) else: print(f"Age set to {age}") #step-3 - handling the custom exception try: set_age(150) except InvalidAgeError as e: print(e) output 150 -> Age must be between 0 and 120 Explanation InvalidAgeError class inherits from Exception. It defines an init method to accept age and message. The str method returns a readable string representation of the error. In set_age(), if the age is outside the valid range (0–120), the exception is raised. The try-except block catches the exception and prints the error message.

When we create a custom exception, we can use sublcass of python's builtin Exception class (or a std subclass like TypeError or ValueError). We can add our own attributes and methods or custom logic to make our exception more informative. Example : We improve the InvalidAgeError by adding error code and customizing the error message. class InvalidAgeError(Exception): def __init__(self, age, msg="Age must be between 0 and 120", error_code=1001): self.age = age self.msg = msg self.error_code = error_code super().__init__(self.msg) def __str__(self): return f"[Error Code {self.error_code}] {self.age} -> {self.msg}" def set_age(age): if age < 0 or age > 120: raise InvalidAgeError(age) else: print(f"Age set to: {age}") try: set_age(150) # This will raise the custom exception except InvalidAgeError as e: print(e) output [Error Code 1001] 150 -> Age must be between 0 and 120 Explanation: InvalidAgeError now has an additional attribute error_code. str method is overridden to display both the error code and the age. When set_age(150) is executed, the exception is raised and caught in the try-except block. The customized error message is printed, making the error more descriptive.

Sometimes instead of directly inherting from Exception, we can create a custom exception by subcalssing a standard exception such as Runtime or ValueError etc. Example: This example shows how to create a custom exception NetworkError by inheriting from RuntimeError, which is a standard bult-in exception. #NetworkError has base RuntimeError and not Exception. class NetworkError(RuntimeError): def __init__(self,arg): self.args=(arg,) #store as tuple try: raise NetworkError("Connection failed") except NetworkError as e: print(e.agrs) output ('Connection failed',) Explanation NetworkError inherits from RuntimeError, which is builtin exception type. When raised the message is stored in the args attribute The exception is caught and its stored arguments are displayed.
documents.s: Using Standard Exception as a Base Class

documents.t: Here is simple example where we raised a custom exception if the email address is not valid. class InvalidEmailError(Exception): def __init__(self,email,msg="Invalid email format"): self.email=email self.msg=msg super().__init__(self.msg) def __str__(self): return f"{self.email} -> {self.msg}" def set_email(email): if "@" not in email: raise invalidEmailError(email) print(f"Email set to :{email}") try: set_email("userexample.com") except InvalidEmailError as e: print(e) output userexample.com -> Invalid email format Explanation: A new exception class InvalidEmailError is defined to validate email addresses. If the given email does not contain "@", the exception is raised. The try-except block catches the error and prints the formatted message.

Representing specific error in an application(eg: InvalidAgeError,DatabaseConnectionError) Providing clearer and more descriptive errro messages Handling a group of related errors separately using except. By uising user- defined exception , program will be more readable,maintainable and easier to debug.

class AppError(Exception): pass class APIError(AppError): pass class AuthError(AppError): pass class PermissionError(AuthError): pass try: raise PermissionError("Access denied") except APIError: print("Handled API error")

class Exception: def __init__(self,*args): self.args=args def __str__(self): if len(self.args)==1: return str(self.args[0]) return str(self.args) When an exception object is created error=Exception("Invalid input"), python collects all args into tuple as error.args #("invalid input,") The str method behaves as follows: print(Exception("Invalid input")) # Invalid input print(Exception("Invalid input", 42)) # ('Invalid input', 42) __str__versus __repr__ The correct method name is repr, not repre. __str__ provides a readable representation intended for users. __repr__ provides a detailed representation intended for developers and debugging. error = ValueError("Invalid age") print(error) # calls str(error), which uses __str__ # Invalid age repr(error) # calls __repr__ # "ValueError('Invalid age')" In an interactive Python console, entering the object directly usually displays its __repr__ result: >>> error ValueError('Invalid age')`

When a new exception is raised while python handling another exception,Python automatically links the new exception to the active exception through __context__. try: int("hello") except ValueError: raise TypeError("Conversion failed") Python is automatically doing something follows type_error.__context__ = value_error The traceback displays ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc' During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred: TypeError: Conversion failed So Implicit chainig does show a relationship: The TypeError occured while handling the ValueError.

Using raise ... from e says that the new exception happened specifically because of e. try: int("abc") except ValueError as e: raise TypeError("Conversion failed") from e Python sets: new_exception.__cause__=e Traceback displays: ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc' The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception: TypeError: Conversion failed try: int("abc") except ValueError as e: try: raise TypeError("Conversion failed") from e except TypeError as ex: print(ex.__cause__ is e) # True print(ex.__context__ is e) # True info context: happened while handling the previous exception. cause: happened because of the previous exception. The distiction is Implicit chaining: new_exception.__context__ = active_exception Explicit chaining: new_exception.__cause__ = specified_exception Traceback messages are try: int("abc") except ValueError as e: try: raise TypeError("Conversion failed") from e except TypeError as ex: print(ex.__cause__ is e) # True print(ex.__context__ is e) # True

Never use a bare except: or overly broad except Exception (You dont know which one to catch). Catch only what you expect and can handle. # ❌ Bad try: data = int(user_input) except: pass # ✅ Good try: data = int(user_input) except ValueError: print("Input must be a number")

A bare pass hides bugs. At minimum, log the error. # ❌ Bad except Exception: pass # ✅ Good import logging except Exception as e: logging.exception("Unexpected error: %s", e) raise # re-raise if you can't recover

finally runs whether or not an exception occurred — ideal for releasing resources. f = open("data.txt") try: process(f) except IOError as e: logging.error("File error: %s", e) finally: f.close() # always runs Even better, use context managers (with) which handle this automatically: with open("data.txt") as f: process(f)

For libraries or large apps, define your own exception hierarchy so callers can catch precisely. class AppError(Exception): """Base exception for this app.""" class DatabaseError(AppError): """Raised on DB failures.""" class ValidationError(AppError): """Raised on invalid input.""" Here for exception we are giving semantic meaning

Preserve the original traceback(Python's way of showing you exactly where and how an error occurred) when wrapping exceptions. try: connect_to_db() except ConnectionRefusedError as e: raise DatabaseError("Could not reach database") from e
documents.s: 5.  Raise with Context — Use raise ... from

documents.t: Exceptions are for exceptional situations, not regular branching logic(if else ). # ❌ Bad (using exceptions as if/else cases) try: value = my_dict[key] except KeyError: value = default # ✅ Good value = my_dict.get(key, default)
documents.s: 6. Avoid Using Exceptions for Flow Control(if else)

documents.t: low-level- exceptions - exceptions that come from the deep technical internals of your program — things like databases, files, networks, and hardware top- The top is the place where your program starts execution — the outermost layer raw- Raw means untranslated, unwrapped, and unprocessed — the exception travels from where it occurred (low level) all the way to the top exactly as it was, with no context added, no explanation, no meaningful message.eg : exception happened at low level but it becomes raw exception at UI level. Most apps have layers: Database → Service → UI # Layer 1 — Database (low level) def fetch_user_from_db(user_id): result = db.query(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id={user_id}") return result # Layer 2 — Service (business logic) def get_user_profile(user_id): return fetch_user_from_db(user_id) # Layer 3 — UI (top level) def show_profile_page(user_id): return get_user_profile(user_id) ❌ Bad — exception bubbles up raw to the top def fetch_user_from_db(user_id): result = db.query(...) # raises OperationalError if DB is down return result def get_user_profile(user_id): return fetch_user_from_db(user_id) # does nothing, just passes it up def show_profile_page(user_id): try: return get_user_profile(user_id) except OperationalError: # caught here at the TOP print("Something went wrong") The UI layer has no idea what OperationalError means in the context of showing a profile page. It can't do anything useful with it. ✅ Good — caught at the right layer # Layer 1 — DB layer catches DB-specific error, wraps it meaningfully def fetch_user_from_db(user_id): try: result = db.query(...) except OperationalError as e: raise DatabaseUnavailableError("DB is down") from e # meaningful! return result # Layer 2 — Service layer catches and handles missing user def get_user_profile(user_id): try: return fetch_user_from_db(user_id) except DatabaseUnavailableError: return None # service knows what to do — return empty profile # Layer 3 — UI only deals with UI concerns def show_profile_page(user_id): user = get_user_profile(user_id) if user is None: print("Profile unavailable, try again later") Each layer handles only what it understands. The raw OperationalError never leaks to the UI Simple rule: Ask yourself — "Does this layer have enough information to do something useful with this error?" If yes, handle it here. If no, let the layer below handle it first.
documents.s: 7. Don't let low-level exceptions()bubble up raw to the top()

documents.t: Use logging.exception() inside an except block — it automatically captures the full traceback. logging.exception() vs logging.error() The only difference is that logging.exception() automatically includes the full traceback, while logging.error() just logs the message you give it. try: data = int("abc") except ValueError as e: logging.exception("Something went wrong") Output: ERROR:root:Something went wrong Traceback (most recent call last): File "app.py", line 2, in <module> data = int("abc") ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc'

Catching bad data at the entry point is cleaner than letting errors propagate deep into logic. def process_age(age: int): if age < 0: raise ValueError(f"Age cannot be negative: {age}") ... info Quick Reference Rule Why Catch specific exceptions Avoids masking unexpected bugs Never silently pass Makes debugging possible Use with / finally Guarantees resource cleanup Custom exception classes Makes error handling precise and readable raise X from Y Preserves full error context Log with logging.exception() Captures full traceback automatically

contextlib.suppress is a context manager that silently ignores specified exceptions — a cleaner alternative to a try/except block where you'd just pass on an error. Basic Usage from contextlib import suppress with suppress(FileNotFoundError): os.remove("file.txt") # No error if file doesn't exist This is equivalent to: try: os.remove("file.txt") except FileNotFoundError: pass
Pass multiple exception types as arguments: with suppress(FileNotFoundError, PermissionError): os.remove("/protected/file.txt")

Safe delete a dict key without KeyError: with suppress(KeyError): del my_dict["optional_key"] Skipping already-existing directory creation: with suppress(FileExistsError): os.mkdir("new_folder") # Though os.makedirs(exist_ok=True) is better here Ignoring StopIteration manually: with suppress(StopIteration): # suppressing StopIteration Error value = next(my_iterator) -> StopIteration my_iterator = iter([1, 2]) next(my_iterator) # 1 next(my_iterator) # 2 next(my_iterator) # 💥 StopIteration — tape is empty If the iterator has another item, that item is assigned to value. If the iterator is exhausted, next() raises StopIteration.

Scenario Prefer You truly don't care about the exception suppress You need to log, inspect, or react to the exception try/except Multiple statements, only one might fail try/except (more precise) to that statement only Clean one-liner "best effort" operation suppress “Clean one-liner” means a small, simple operation where ignoring one specific exception is not a problem. info Execution stops at the exception — code after the failing line ,inside the with block is skipped. with suppress(ValueError): x = int("bad") # raises → suppressed and going out of the with block print("skipped") # this never runs
documents.s: When to use suppress vs try/except

documents.t: Here is the breakdown of what happend when you define and execute a function.

When we call a function, python creates Frame object(also called Stack Frame) and pushes onto Stack memory in runtime. A stack frame is a temporary chunk of memory that contains everything the function needs to run : Local variable Array : Fast accessing local variables defined inside the function. Evaluation stack : Where intermediate values are stored during calculations. References to code object : The compiled Byte code. Global pointer : a link to the module's global namespace Back to pointer : a link to the frame that called this function.(eg : if inside function1 , it is calling function2 , then function2's back to pointer will be function1.) When a function returns its frame will be popped off the stack memory and its local variable are totaly destroyed. NOTE: - For inspecting the frames use python's sys module.Calling sys._getframe() will gives currently executing frame object.
documents.s: 1- Stack Frames: How python executes a function

documents.t: Python uses LEGB rule to check variable names. Namesapce are checked from the inside to out.The point python finds a match it stops looking. Scope Description Local Variable defined in the current function Enclosing Variable in the local scope of any enclosing/outer function(incase of nested function) Global variable defined at the beginning of the module,or declared global inside the function. Built-in Names preloaded into python(like print, len,ValueError)
documents.s: 2. LEGB Scope : How to Python looks up variables

documents.t: Python doesn't lookup local variable by searching dictionary at runtime.Because python knows the local variable at compile time (explains later), and python store them in fixed array within stack frame at run time.Then interpretr fetches the the value of local variables using brilliant fast instruction called LOAD_FAST. But for Global and Built-in variables will change dynamically (values can be updated while program running, or variable can be added or deleted completely from global space.), so python has look up in dictionaries using LOAD_GLOBAL which is slightly slower.
documents.s: OPtimisation under the hood : LOAD_FAST vs LOAD_GLOBAL

documents.t: A closure happens when a nested function references a variable from its enclosing(outer) scope, even that outer function finishes executing. def make_multiplier(x): def multiplier(y): return x * y # 'x' is a free variable return multiplier times_three = make_multiplier(3) print(times_three(5)) # Outputs 15 Normally Outer function exits, the stack frame will be destroyed.So does the inner function still access those variable? CEll objects. The Magic : Cell object​ when python compiles make_multiplier ,it see the inner function has used the x , instead storing x in stack frame , Python wraps it in a special internal object called Cell Object. The Cell object lives in the heap independent of the stack frame. Both the outer function and inner function will store a reference to the cell object. Even after the outer function make_multiplier's stack frame vanishes, the inner function keeps refrence to the cell object via __closure__ attribute. closure attribute closure attribute is hidden , built-in property found on every python object.It is a tuple of cell objects, where each cell contains a reference to a variable from outer scope that the function needs to remember. if a function not using any varibales from the outer scope its __closure__ attribute is None.
documents.s: 3. Closures : Free Variables and Cell objects

documents.t: *args and kwargs accepts a variable number of arguments. *args (Positional arguments): Python collects all excess positional arguments and pack them into a tuple, and assigns it to a variable name args kwargs (Keyworded arguments): Python collects all excess keyworded arguments , pack them into a dictionary, and assigns a variable name kwargs. Becuase of tuple are immutable and cheaper to allocate than lists, Python striclty uses tuple for *args to keep function calls memory effecient.

In python functions are class objects. When we write a function , Python compiles source code into code object (<class 'code'>), which is attached to the function as __code__ attribute. code objects contains the raw byte code and metadata to build stack frame. NOTE : at compile time code object only created , which is blueprint for stack frame, stack frame will be created at runtime. __code__ properties def example_func(a, b=10): secret = "internal" return a + b co_varnames a tuple containing all local variable names (parameters followed by local variables) NOTE : Cell object is diffrent , that is the case of nested function , but this is propery of code object. print(example_func.__code__.co_varnames) # (a,b,secret) co_argcount The number of positional arguments(including those with default values) print(example_func.__code__.co_argcount) # 2 co_consts A tuple of all literal constants used in the function. (Python stores these here so it doesn't have to recreate them). "Literal constants" include things like numbers (10, 3.14), strings ("hello"), booleans (True, False), and None. why None : In Python, every function must return something. If a function reaches the end of its block without hitting an explicit return statement, or if it executes a bare return, it automatically returns None. Because None is a literal value that the Python interpreter might need to load and return, the compiler automatically injects it into the co_consts tuple just in case. print(example_func.__code__.co_consts) # (None, 10, 'internal') co_code: The raw, unadulterated bytecode string. print(example_func.__code__.co_code) # b'\x97\x00...' (raw bytes) Peeking at the Bytecode with dis​ To make sense of co_code, you can use the built-in dis (disassembler) module to turn those raw bytes into human-readable assembly instructions: import dis dis.dis(example_func) output 2 0 RESUME 0 3 2 LOAD_CONST 2 ('internal') 4 STORE_FAST 2 (secret) 4 6 LOAD_FAST 0 (a) 8 LOAD_FAST 1 (b) 10 BINARY_OP 0 (+) 14 RETURN_VALUE

To make sense of co_code, you can use the built-in dis (disassembler) module to turn those raw bytes into human-readable assembly instructions: import dis dis.dis(example_func) output 2 0 RESUME 0 3 2 LOAD_CONST 2 ('internal') 4 STORE_FAST 2 (secret) 4 6 LOAD_FAST 0 (a) 8 LOAD_FAST 1 (b) 10 BINARY_OP 0 (+) 14 RETURN_VALUE
documents.s: Peeking at the Bytecode with dis

documents.t: Global interpreter lock(GIL) is a lock used in CPython interpreter(the default and most widely used Python Implentaion). It ensures only one thread excecute at a time even if you have multiple CPU cores(multithreading case , not for multiprocessing case). Python supports Mulithreading , but threads can't run CPU-bound code parallel inside CPython. However, I\O-bound multitreading does work well because threads allowed to release the GIL while waiting for I\O operations.

In CPython, every python object uses reference countuing for memory management. import sys a = "Geek" print(sys.getrefcount(a)) b = a print(sys.getrefcount(a)) Issue : If two threads simultaneosly modify the reference count, it can cause: corrupted memory,inconsistent object states and crashes. Possible solution : Cpython could lock every object or memory update from different threads, but that would make Python much slower. Final decision : Introduce one GIL, Cpython designers add one single interpreter-wide lock,the GIL which guarantees: Thread-safe management Simplicity of implementation Fast perfomance for single-threaded program

CPython uses C at the core,most extensions(NumPy,PIL etc) are written in C.C doesn't automatically manages memory.hence CPython implements its own reference counting.The GIL made memory management simple Perfomance boost for single-threaded program,A single lock is faster than thousands of fine-grained (to avoid race conditions) locks. Ease of implementation, CPython was designed in the early 1990s when multi-core processors were rare. The GIL was an easy and safe solution.
documents.s: Factors that led to the GIL

documents.t: Depending on your workload,the GIL has different effects.
documents.s: Impact of GIL on Multi-threaded Programs

documents.t: Program that do heavy calculations:loops,math,compression,hasing,etc are CPU-bound operations. In CPU-bound task,multiple threads do NOT run in parallel because the GIL doesn't allow it. ** Note**: The execution time varies depending on the hardware, operating system, Python version, and current system workload. import time COUNT = 50_000_000 def countdown(n): while n > 0: n -= 1 start = time.time() countdown(COUNT) print("Time taken:", time.time() - start) ouptut Time taken: <varies by system> Explanation: This example performs a CPU-intensive countdown operation using a single thread. It serves as a baseline for comparing the effect of multithreading on CPU-bound workloads.

Despite of 2 threads , performance is almost the same because only one thread runs bytecode at ANY moment. import time from threading import Thread COUNT = 50_000_000 def countdown(n): while n > 0: n -= 1 t1 = Thread(target=countdown, args=(COUNT//2,)) t2 = Thread(target=countdown, args=(COUNT//2,)) start = time.time() t1.start(); t2.start() t1.join(); t2.join() print("Time taken:", time.time() - start) output Time taken: <varies by system> Explanation: Although two threads are used, the GIL prevents them from executing Python bytecode simultaneously. As a result, CPU-bound tasks typically see little or no performance improvement from multithreading.
documents.s: 2. CPU-bound with two threads(GIL blocks true parallelism)

documents.t: Program that wait for I\O: downloading files,reading files,database quiries,API calls. Here doen't slow you down, because When a threard waits for I\O ->it releases the GIL Other threads can run during the time. So I\O bounded tasks works well in python.

Too many libraries depend on it : Numpy,Pandas,OpenCV and thousands of C-extension packages rely on the GIL. WIthout GIL, Python would slow down: Many small locks would make Python slower for normal programs. Compatibility issues: Removing GIL breaks how existing python code works Previous attempts failed: They made python slower and used more memory However, Python 3.13 introduces optional builds without the GIL, but it is still experimental.

Each Process has it own interpreter and GIL,so they truly run in parallel. import multprocessing import time COUNT=5000000 def count(n): while n>0: n-=1 if __name__=="__main__": p1=multprocessing.Process(target=countdown,args=(COUNT//2)) p2=multiprocessing.Process(target=countdown,args=(COUNT//2)) start=time.time() p1.start(),p2.start() p1.join(),p2.join() print(f"Time taken- {start-time.time()}") This uses multiple CPU Cores.
NumPy OpenCV Pandas(some operations) SciPy NOte: These libraries runs heavy computations in C and releases the GIL,allowing parallel execution.
documents.s: 2. Use libraries that releases the GIL

documents.t: asyncio doesn't use multithreads.It uses a single thred that switches between tasks efficeintly. Great for API calls network tasks database operations

Generator is special type of function that returns an iterator object. Insted of using return keyword to send single value, generator function uses yeild to produce series of values overtime. The Generator function pauses its execution after yield, maintaining (retaining or freezes) its state between iterations. Each call to next() on generator object , resumes its execution right afetr the yield statement. Example def func(max): cnt=1 while cnt <=max: yield cnt cnt+=1 ctr=func(5) for n in ctr: print(n) output 1 2 3 4 5
Handle large or infinite data without loading everything into memory.
Yield items one by one at a time ,avoiding full list creation.
Lazy evaluation measns "Dont do the work, dont use the memory until the code absolutely asks for it". So only while calling next() , then next iteration happens.
Ideal for generating unbounded data like Fibonacci series.

Chaining generators (built by passing one generator into another.) , to process data in stages efficeintly. The scinario cleaning a list of text cleain up extra spaces Capitalize the names Keep only the names start with the letter 'A' Here is how the generator chains together. # Our raw data source raw_names = [" alice ", " bob ", " charlie ", " alexandRA "] # Stage 1: Strips white spaces strip_stage = (name.strip() for name in raw_names) # Stage 2: Capitalizes the words caps_stage = (name.capitalize() for name in strip_stage) # Stage 3: Filters only 'A' names final_pipeline = (name for name in caps_stage if name.startswith("A")) # --- AT THIS POINT: Zero work has been done. --- # The pipeline is built, but nobody has asked for data yet. print("Starting the loop...") for name in final_pipeline: print(f"Result: {name}") The data moves as "Pull effect". When the for name in fina_pipeline loop runs, it pulls from the end of the line.Here is the exact chain reaction. [Loop] says: "Hey Final Stage, give me a name!" ↓ [Final Stage (Filter 'A')] says: "I don't have one yet. Hey Caps Stage, give me a capitalized name!" ↓ [Caps Stage] says: "I don't have one either. Hey Strip Stage, strip something for me!" ↓ [Strip Stage] grabs " alice " from the raw list, strips it to "alice", and hands it to Caps Stage. ↓ [Caps Stage] takes "alice", turns it into "Alice", and hands it to the Final Stage. ↓ [Final Stage] checks "Alice". It starts with 'A'! It hands it to the Loop. ↓ [Loop] prints: "Result: Alice" Why this is highly efficeint​ If you did this with regular python list, Python would: Loop through all names to strip and save a new list to memory. Loop through all stripped names to capitalizes them and save another new list to memory Loop through all the capitalized names to filter them and save a third list to memory. With Generator Pipeline, there are no intermediate lists are created in computer's RAM.It is a single continues stream where one item goes all the way through the pipeline before next item even enters.

Yeild :​ It is used in generator function to provide a sequence of values over time.When yield is executed it pauses the function, returns the current value and retains the state of the function.This allows the functions to continues from same point when called again through next(), making it ideal for generating large or complex sequences efficeinlty. Return:​ It is used to exit from the function and return a final value.Once return is executed , function is terminated immediately and no state is retained.This is suitabel for cases where single value is neede from a function.

It is used in generator function to provide a sequence of values over time.When yield is executed it pauses the function, returns the current value and retains the state of the function.This allows the functions to continues from same point when called again through next(), making it ideal for generating large or complex sequences efficeinlty.

It is used to exit from the function and return a final value.Once return is executed , function is terminated immediately and no state is retained.This is suitabel for cases where single value is neede from a function.

Generator expression are concise(short) way to create generators. They are similar to list comprehensation but use parentheses instead of square brackets and more memory efficeint. Example sq=(x*x for x in range(1,5)) for in sq: print(i) output 1 4 9 16

yield from​ yield from is a shortcut used inside the generator to yield all values from another iterable (list,tuple, or another generrator) without using the for loop. old way def combine_lists_old(): list_a = [1, 2] list_b = [3, 4] # We have to loop through list_a manually for num in list_a: yield num # We have to loop through list_b manually for num in list_b: yield num # Test it for x in combine_lists_old(): print(x) # Outputs: 1, 2, 3, 4 The cleaner way def combine_lists_new(): list_a = [1, 2] list_b = [3, 4] # Shortcut! "Yield everything inside list_a, then move on" yield from list_a yield from list_b # Test it for x in combine_lists_new(): print(x) # Outputs: 1, 2, 3, 4 WHY USEFULL Saves line of code Perfect for flatteinig the data If you have nested structures (like a list of lists) or chained generators in a pipeline, yield from lets you seamlessly pass data up the chain without nesting multiple for loops send() Method​ Normally generator only give data using ```yield`` . The send() method make them 2 way street It allows to inject a data value back inside the generator exactly where it last paused The value we send actually becomes the result of the yield statement inside the function. def dynamic_counter(): count = 0 while True: # 1. Python pauses right here when it hits 'yield count' and outputs the value. # 2. When you use .send(), the received value drops into the 'received' variable. received= yeild count # If the user sent something, update our count! if received is not None: count = received else: count += 1 # --- Using the generator --- gen = dynamic_counter() # STEP 1: The Prime Rule (You must always start/prime a generator) # You can't send data immediately because the generator hasn't paused at a 'yield' yet. print(next(gen)) # Outputs: 0 (Pauses at the first yield) # STEP 2: Let it run normally print(next(gen)) # Outputs: 1 (received is None, so it increments by 1) print(next(gen)) # Outputs: 2 # STEP 3: Use .send() to inject a value! # This changes 'received' to 100 inside the function print(gen.send(100)) # Outputs: 100 # STEP 4: Continue normally from the new starting point print(next(gen)) # Outputs: 101

yield fromfor old way def combine_lists_old(): list_a = [1, 2] list_b = [3, 4] # We have to loop through list_a manually for num in list_a: yield num # We have to loop through list_b manually for num in list_b: yield num # Test it for x in combine_lists_old(): print(x) # Outputs: 1, 2, 3, 4 The cleaner way def combine_lists_new(): list_a = [1, 2] list_b = [3, 4] # Shortcut! "Yield everything inside list_a, then move on" yield from list_a yield from list_b # Test it for x in combine_lists_new(): print(x) # Outputs: 1, 2, 3, 4 WHY USEFULL Saves line of code Perfect for flatteinig the data If you have nested structures (like a list of lists) or chained generators in a pipeline, yield from lets you seamlessly pass data up the chain without nesting multiple for loops

You must "prime"(start) it first​ When we create a generator it is sitting at the top level of the function.It has not hit a yield statement yet. You must call a next(gen) or gen.send(None) before doing the .send(value) .Otherwise if you first call gen.send(100) will raise TypeError. The yield statement should come first for catching it ,before we sending. If there is no yeild statement then it will crashes with TypeError. next(gen) is just a short call​ Calling next(gen) is same as calling gen.send(None) Application:​ In advanced programming, .send() is the foundational building block for coroutines and light weight cooperative multitasking. It allows different parts of the program to pause, pass data back and forth, coordinate tasks without running multiple heavy threads at the same time.

Useful for procesing large files like logs, because generator handle it in smaller parts ,only holds one logs in memory at any given second, saving memory and we dont wants to load the entire file at once. The iterator can do similar tasks, Generators are quicker bcz of we dont wants define next and iter methods manually.

Iterator : A generic concept (a python object) used to loop over the sequence.To make custom iterator , we have to build a class with two methods __iter__() and __next__().Also we need to manage the internal state. Generaot : A simple way to create an iterator.Instead of writing the whole class , just write a regular function with yield keyword instead of return .Python automatically handles __iter__() and __next__() mechanism for you behind the scenes.
