Feature Iterator Generator Implementation Class based (requires __next__() and __iter__()) Function based (uses keyword yield) State Management Manual (You to tarck variables using self) Automatic (Python saves the local variable state) Complexity More boilerplate code: Harder Clean,readable and highly Pythonic Memory efficeincy High(Compues values on the fly(only when actually needed)) High(Computes values on the fly(only when actually needed)) NOTE : Iterator is different from Iterable Iterable(The Book) : The actual data collection(list, tuple or set).It contains all data ,it holds millions of records in the Computer memory, It is not memory efficeint. Iterator(The Bookmark) : This is a separate tiny object whose only job to point to one specific spot in a collection and know how to move to the next one.So Memory efficeint.

a. The iterator way(Class)​ We ahve to track the state (self.current) yourself and manually raise StopIteration excpetion when done. class CountUp: def __init__(self,max_num): self.max_num=max_num self.current=1 def __iter__(self): return self def __next__(): if self.current <=self.max_num: result=self.current self.current+=1 return result else: raise StopIteration b. The Generator way (Function)​ Everytime Python hits a yield keyword, it pauses the function execution, saves its state ,and spit out the value.When called again, it resumes right where it left off. def count_up(max_num): current=1 while current <=max_num: yield current current+=1 -> How use you Both # They are used exactly the same way! for num in count_up(3): print(num) # Outputs: 1, 2, 3
documents.s: C. Side by Side Code Example

documents.t: Python handles memory automatically using mechanism like Reference couting and garbage collection.

This is the primary memory management of the python. Every object keeps a reference counter, which tells how many variables are currently pointing to that object. When a new refrence to that object (variable) is created, then counter increases. When a reference is deleted or out of scope, counter decreases. When counter is zero, then no variable is using that object, so python (Garbage collector) automatically deallocates(frees) that memory.

It is a process in which python automatically frees memory occupied by the objects that are no longer in use. Fixing reference cycles​ Suppose Object A pointing to Object B and Vice versa, then thier reference count will never drops to zero - causing memory leak. To fix this python uses GC. GC uses a mechanism called Heuristics - When a number of allocation minus number of deallocation exceeds a certain threshold, the GC triggers a collection sweep to break the cycles and free the trapped memory. **

The process of reserving the space in computer's memory so that a program can store its data and variable while it runs. In ptyhon this process is automatically doing by interpreter. There is a big difference in performance depends on how the objects are stored and reused. Performace optimisation​ Object Pooling (Small Integer and String Caching)​ Python does an inernal optimisation in memory called - object interning for small immutable objects (like integers -5 to 256 and short strings). Instead of creating a new object (immutable objects) everytime they will reuse the same object to save memory. a = 10 b = 10 print(a is b) # True (They point to the exact same memory address!) a = 300 b = 300 print(a is b) # False (Outside the cached range, so two separate objects are made) GIL​ Python's Memory management is not thread thread safe , So only one thread executes byte code at a time.

Memory(RAM) is divided mainly into 2 parts Stack Memory​ This is the place where varible are store when python calls a function for temporary, once the function stops executing the stack memory used by these variables will automatically freed. Allocation happens in continues block of memory. def func(): # These variables are created in stack memory a=4 b=[] c='' When func() is called a,b,c are stoed in memory.As soon as function ends this memory will release automatically. Heap Memory​ Heap Memory is where actual objects and values are stored. When a variable is created , its object will store in heap memory. Stack memory stores only the reference(pointer) to this object. Objects in heaps memory can be share among multiple methods or exist even after the has finished executing. This is where large data structures are stored Garbage collection is responsible for cleaning up unused objects from heap memory. Difference b/w Stack and Heap Heap memory is the storage area where all the objects/values are live and Stack memory is the references to them. Heap is more flexible and Stack follows order.

Memory Profiling is the process of analyzing a program's memory consumption while it is running. The goal is to find how much memory is being used and where it is (which line of code) being spent. Memory profiling helps to solve 2 issues Memory Leak - memory no longer alive byt trapped eg: trapped in reference cycle. High Memory Bloat - Code isn't memory leaking but it is incredibly inefficent. eg : loading 4gb csv into a python list instead of streaming it line by line. Tools used forMemory Profiling​ tracemalloc Introduced in python 3.4 Built in module used to track memory allocation in python. It calls into python's internal memory allocators and record eaxctly which file and which line of code allocated piece of memory. Key Capabilities Traceback - It tells exact which file and line of code that created a specific object. Snapshots - You can take picture of memory state at different times and compare them to see exactly what happens. Statics - It can provide high level summaries of total memory usage grouped by file name. Code example import tracemalloc #starting tracking memory allocation tracemalloc.start() # Take initial snapshot of the memory sanpshot1= tracemalloc.take_snapshot() # stimulate some code that consumes memory def create_bolt(): bloated_list=[x for x in range(100000)] return bloated_list my_data= create_blot() # Take second snapshot of the memory snapshot2= tracemalloc.take_snapshot() #step- 3- Compare the 2 snapshots top_stats= snapshot2.compare_to(snapshot1, 'lineno') #Printing top3 memory consuming lines for stat i top_stats[:3]: print(stat) output [ Top 3 Memory Consuming Lines ] memory_profling.py:12: size=35.0 MiB (+35.0 MiB), count=999744 (+999744), average=37 B memory_profling.py:7: size=576 B (+576 B), count=1 (+1), average=576 B memory_profling.py:10: size=136 B (+136 B), count=1 (+1), average=136 B This tells memory_profiling line 12 is responsible for holding 35 mb of memory across roughly 1 million individual allocations.

tracemalloc Introduced in python 3.4 Built in module used to track memory allocation in python. It calls into python's internal memory allocators and record eaxctly which file and which line of code allocated piece of memory. Key Capabilities Traceback - It tells exact which file and line of code that created a specific object. Snapshots - You can take picture of memory state at different times and compare them to see exactly what happens. Statics - It can provide high level summaries of total memory usage grouped by file name. Code example import tracemalloc #starting tracking memory allocation tracemalloc.start() # Take initial snapshot of the memory sanpshot1= tracemalloc.take_snapshot() # stimulate some code that consumes memory def create_bolt(): bloated_list=[x for x in range(100000)] return bloated_list my_data= create_blot() # Take second snapshot of the memory snapshot2= tracemalloc.take_snapshot() #step- 3- Compare the 2 snapshots top_stats= snapshot2.compare_to(snapshot1, 'lineno') #Printing top3 memory consuming lines for stat i top_stats[:3]: print(stat) output [ Top 3 Memory Consuming Lines ] memory_profling.py:12: size=35.0 MiB (+35.0 MiB), count=999744 (+999744), average=37 B memory_profling.py:7: size=576 B (+576 B), count=1 (+1), average=576 B memory_profling.py:10: size=136 B (+136 B), count=1 (+1), average=136 B

Multiprocessing refers to the abilty of a sysytem support more than one processor at a time. Application in multiprocessing is broken to smaller routines and run independantly The os allocates these theads into processors ,improving performace of the system.

A process is a program in execution. When we write source code and compile it,the compiler generates an executable binary file.This executable file is called a program.When the program is loaded into the memory and executed it become a process A process is an 'active' entity instead of a program, which is considered as a 'passive' entity. A single program can create many processes when run multiple times.

A process in memory is divided into several distinct sections, each serving different purpose. Text section : A text or code segment contains executable instructions.it is read only sectiion. stack : The stack contains temporary data,such as function parameters return address and local variables. Data section: Contains global variable. Heap section : Dynamically memory allocated to process during its run time.

A process has several important attributes that helps the OS to manage and control it. These attributes are stored in a structure called ProcessControlBLock(PCB). Process ID (PID): A unique number assigned to each process so the OS can identify it. Process state : This shows the current state of the process like running,waiting or ready to execute. Priority and other CPU Scheduling Information: Data that helps the OS to decide which process runs next, like priority levels and pointers to scheduling queues. I\O Infromation:Information about the input and output device the process is using. File Descriptors: Information about open files and network connections. Accounting information: Tracks how long process has run,the amoun of CPU time used,and other resource usage data. Memory managaement information: Details about the memory state allocated to the process,including where it is loaded in the memory and its structure of its memory layout(stack,heap etc.)

Modern system are designed to handle multiple tasks effectively while maintaining good performance and responsiveness.These are achieved through the threads and processes. Process is the independent program with its own memory. Threads is small unit of process sharing same memory. Process: A program that is currently in execution within the OS.It operates in an indepedent environment and managing by the OS. Process communicates each other using Inter-Process Communication(IPC) methods. Thread :This is Smallest unit of execution within a process.It enables a program to perform multiple tasks concurrently while sharing the same memory and resource. Each thread had TID for identification Like Proccess , thread also move to the states such as new,runnable,running,waiting and terminated. Threads within the same process shares memory and resources,enabling faster communication. Context switching can occur between threads to allow multiple tasks to execute effectively.

Units of Execution : Both are execution units in OS and are part of process management. OS Scheduling & Preemption : Both are scheduled by the OS for fair CPU allocation and can be preempted for multitasking. Execution context : Each has its own execution context,including program counter,CPU registers and satck space. Creation during Runtime : Both can create child entities during their execution lifecycle. Communication and Resource Release : Both can communicate using IPC mechanisms, and upton termination their allocated resource are released back to the OS.

Process Thread Program in execution Part of process Takes more time to create and terminate Takes less time to create and terminate Context switching is slow Context switching is fast Heavyweight Lightweight Less efficient communication More efficient communication Blocking one process doesn't affect others Blocking a user level(I\O operations) thread may block all Uses system calls Created using APIs has its own PCB,stack,address space shares PCB and addresss space ,has own TCB and stack Doesn't share data Shares data with other threads

If a system has only one processor, and it assigns several processes at a time,it will have to interrupt each task and switch to another,to keep all of the process is going. A multiprocessig system can have: Multiprocessor: ie , a computer with more than one processor Here CPU can easily execute several tasks at once,with each task using its own processor.

# importing the multiprocessing module import multiprocessing def print_cube(num): """ function to print cube of given num """ print("Cube: {}".format(num * num * num)) def print_square(num): """ function to print square of given num """ print("Square: {}".format(num * num)) if __name__ == "__main__": # creating processes p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=print_square, args=(10, )) p2 = multiprocessing.Process(target=print_cube, args=(10, )) # starting process 1 p1.start() # starting process 2 p2.start() # wait until process 1 is finished p1.join() # wait until process 2 is finished p2.join() # both processes finished print("Done!") To import multiprocessing import multiprocessing target : the function to be executed by process args : the arguments to be passed to the target function To start a process we use start method of process class Inorder to stop the execution of current program until a program is complete, we use join method. Example: The concept of different process running on same python script. # importing the multiprocessing module import multiprocessing import os def worker1(): # printing process id print("ID of process running worker1: {}".format(os.getpid())) def worker2(): # printing process id print("ID of process running worker2: {}".format(os.getpid())) if __name__ == "__main__": # printing main program process id print("ID of main process: {}".format(os.getpid())) # creating processes p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=worker1) p2 = multiprocessing.Process(target=worker2) # starting processes p1.start() p2.start() # process IDs print("ID of process p1: {}".format(p1.pid)) print("ID of process p2: {}".format(p2.pid)) # wait until processes are finished p1.join() p2.join() # both processes finished print("Both processes finished execution!") # check if processes are alive print("Process p1 is alive: {}".format(p1.is_alive())) print("Process p2 is alive: {}".format(p2.is_alive())) output ID of main process: 28628 ID of process running worker1: 29305 ID of process running worker2: 29306 ID of process p1: 29305 ID of process p2: 29306 Both processes finished execution! Process p1 is alive: False Process p2 is alive: False The main process has a different process ID and multiprocessing module spawns new processes with different pids as we create Process objects p1 and p2. os.getpid() : It is used to get ID of the process running in the current target function. Ecah process runs indepedentaly and has its own memory space. As soon as execution of target function is finished,the process get terminated.We use is_alive() to check if the process is still active or not.

In multiprocessing, any newly created process will do the following, run indepedently have their own memory space import multiprocessing # empty list with global scope result = [] def square_list(mylist): """ function to square a given list """ global result # append squares of mylist to global list result for num in mylist: result.append(num * num) # print global list result print("Result(in process p1): {}".format(result)) if __name__ == "__main__": # input list mylist = [1,2,3,4] # creating new process p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=square_list, args=(mylist,)) # starting process p1.start() # wait until process is finished p1.join() # print global result list print("Result(in main program): {}".format(result)) output Result(in process p1): [1, 4, 9, 16] Result(in main program): [] in square_list,Since this function is called by process p1,"result" list is changed in the memory space of process p1 only. Since main program is run by a different process, its memory space still contains empty list.

Multiprocessing module provides Array and Value objects to share data between processes. Array : a ctypes array allocated from shared memory. Value : a ctypes object allocated from the shared memory. ctypes : It is a builtin python module that provides C-compatible data types. import multiprocessing def square_list(mylist, result, square_sum): """ function to square a given list """ # append squares of mylist to result array for idx, num in enumerate(mylist): result[idx] = num * num # square_sum value square_sum.value = sum(result) # print result Array print("Result(in process p1): {}".format(result[:])) # print square_sum Value print("Sum of squares(in process p1): {}".format(square_sum.value)) if __name__ == "__main__": # input list mylist = [1,2,3,4] # creating Array of int data type with space for 4 integers result = multiprocessing.Array('i', 4) # creating Value of int data type square_sum = multiprocessing.Value('i') # creating new process p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=square_list, args=(mylist, result, square_sum)) # starting process p1.start() # wait until the process is finished p1.join() # print result array print("Result(in main program): {}".format(result[:])) # print square_sum Value print("Sum of squares(in main program): {}".format(square_sum.value)) output Result(in process p1): [1, 4, 9, 16] Sum of squares(in process p1): 30 Result(in main program): [1, 4, 9, 16] Sum of squares(in main program): 30 First we create Array result like this, result=multiprocessing.Array('i',4) First argument is the datatype ,'i' stands for integer whereas 'd' stands for float datan type Secong argument is the size of the array. We Create Value square_sum , square_sum=multiprocessing.Value('i') Here we only needs specify data type,we can give deafult value as like this square_sum=multiprocessing.Value('i',10) result array elements are given a value by specifying index of array element. for idx, num in enumerate(mylist): result[idx] = num * num square_sum is given a value by using its value attribute square_sum.value = sum(result)

Multiprocessing supports two types of communication channel between processes: Queue Pipe 1.Queue​ A simple way to communicate between process with multiprocessing is to use a Queue to pass messages back and forth. Any Python object can pass through a Queue. Note : multiprocessing.Queue class is a near clone of queue.queue import multiprocessing def square_list(mylist, q): """ function to square a given list """ # append squares of mylist to queue for num in mylist: q.put(num * num) def print_queue(q): """ function to print queue elements """ print("Queue elements:") while not q.empty(): print(q.get()) print("Queue is now empty!") if __name__ == "__main__": # input list mylist = [1,2,3,4] # creating multiprocessing Queue q = multiprocessing.Queue() # creating new processes p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=square_list, args=(mylist, q)) p2 = multiprocessing.Process(target=print_queue, args=(q,)) # running process p1 to square list p1.start() p1.join() # running process p2 to get queue elements p2.start() p2.join() output Queue elements: 1 4 9 16 Queue is now empty! Elemenets are inserted into the queue using put method In order to print queue elements we use get method until queue is not empty. 2.Pipes​ A pipe can have only 2 endpoints.Hence it is preferred over queue when only two-way communication is required. Multiprocessing module provides Pipe() function which returns a pair of connection objects connected by a pipe. The two objects returned by the Pipe function represents the 2 ends of the pipe. Each connection object has send() and recv() methods. import multiprocessing def sender(conn, msgs): """ function to send messages to other end of pipe """ for msg in msgs: conn.send(msg) print("Sent the message: {}".format(msg)) conn.close() def receiver(conn): """ function to print the messages received from other end of pipe """ while 1: msg = conn.recv() if msg == "END": break print("Received the message: {}".format(msg)) if __name__ == "__main__": # messages to be sent msgs = ["hello", "hey", "hru?", "END"] # creating a pipe parent_conn, child_conn = multiprocessing.Pipe() # creating new processes p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=sender, args=(parent_conn,msgs)) p2 = multiprocessing.Process(target=receiver, args=(child_conn,)) # running processes p1.start() p2.start() # wait until processes finish p1.join() p2.join() output Sent the message: hello Sent the message: hey Sent the message: hru? Received the message: hello Sent the message: END Received the message: hey Received the message: hru? Message is send from one end of pipe to another using send method. To receive any messages at one end of the pipe, we use recv method. NOte : Data in a pipe may become corrupted if two processes(or threads) try to read from or write to the same end of the pipe at the same time.Of course, there is no risk of corruption from processes using different ends of the pipe at the same time. Also note that queues do proper synchronisation between processes at the expense of more complexity.Hence queues are said to be Thread and Process safe.

Process synchronization is defined as a mechanism which ensures that two or more concurrent process do not simultaneously execute some particluar program segment known as critical section. Critical section : it refers to the parts of the program where the shared resource is accessed. Concurrent access to shared resource can lead to race condition Race condition : A race condition occurs when two or more processes can access shared data and they try to change it at the same time. As a result, the values of variables may be unpredictable and vary depending on the timings of context switches of the processes. Example showing race condition # Python program to illustrate # the concept of race condition # in multiprocessing import multiprocessing # function to withdraw from account def withdraw(balance): for _ in range(10000): balance.value = balance.value - 1 # function to deposit to account def deposit(balance): for _ in range(10000): balance.value = balance.value + 1 def perform_transactions(): # initial balance (in shared memory) balance = multiprocessing.Value('i', 100) # creating new processes p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=withdraw, args=(balance,)) p2 = multiprocessing.Process(target=deposit, args=(balance,)) # starting processes p1.start() p2.start() # wait until processes are finished p1.join() p2.join() # print final balance print("Final balance = {}".format(balance.value)) if __name__ == "__main__": for _ in range(10): # perform same transaction process 10 times perform_transactions() output Final balance = 1311 Final balance = 199 Final balance = 558 Final balance = -2265 Final balance = 1371 Final balance = 1158 Final balance = -577 Final balance = -1300 Final balance = -341 Final balance = 157 This happens due to concurrent access of processes to shared shared data balance.This unpredictability in balance is race condition.

Multiprocessing module provide a Lock class to deal with the race conditions. Lock is implemented using semaphore object provided by OS. Semaphore object is simply a value in a designated place in OS storage that each process can check and then change. Depending on the value that is found,the process can use the resource or will find that is already in use and must wait for some periods before trying again. Semphore can be binary(0 or 1) or can have additional values. Typically a process using semaphores checks the value and then, if it using the resource changes the value to reflect this so that subsequent semaphore users will know to wait. Example # Python program to illustrate # the concept of locks # in multiprocessing import multiprocessing # function to withdraw from account def withdraw(balance, lock): for _ in range(10000): lock.acquire() balance.value = balance.value - 1 lock.release() # function to deposit to account def deposit(balance, lock): for _ in range(10000): lock.acquire() balance.value = balance.value + 1 lock.release() def perform_transactions(): # initial balance (in shared memory) balance = multiprocessing.Value('i', 100) # creating a lock object lock = multiprocessing.Lock() # creating new processes p1 = multiprocessing.Process(target=withdraw, args=(balance,lock)) p2 = multiprocessing.Process(target=deposit, args=(balance,lock)) # starting processes p1.start() p2.start() # wait until processes are finished p1.join() p2.join() # print final balance print("Final balance = {}".format(balance.value)) if __name__ == "__main__": for _ in range(10): # perform same transaction process 10 times perform_transactions() output Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 Final balance = 100 lock object is created, and then it is passed as the argument of target function. In the critcal section of target function, we apply lock using lock.acquire() method.As soon as a lock is acquired, no other process can access its critical section until the lock is released using lock.release() method.

For normal programs usually ,only one of the core is used for program execution and its quite possible that other cores remains idle. In order to utilize all the core multiprocessing module gives Pool class. The Pool class represents a pool of worker processes. The tasks is offloaded/distributed among the cores/processes automatically by Pool object. User doesn't need to worry about creating processes explicitly. example # Python program to understand # the concept of pool import multiprocessing import os def square(n): print("Worker process id for {0}: {1}".format(n, os.getpid())) return (n*n) if __name__ == "__main__": # input list mylist = [1,2,3,4,5] # creating a pool object p = multiprocessing.Pool() # map list to target function result = p.map(square, mylist) print(result) output Worker process id for 2: 4152 Worker process id for 1: 4151 Worker process id for 4: 4151 Worker process id for 3: 4153 Worker process id for 5: 4152 [1, 4, 9, 16, 25] There are few arguments for gaining more control over offloading of task.These are processes: specify the number of worker processes. maxtasksperchild: specify the maximum number of task to be assigned per child process. The relationship, Process ├── Thread 1 │ ├── Task A │ ├── Task B │ └── Task C │ ├── Thread 2 │ ├── Task D │ └── Task E │ └── Thread 3 └── Task F All the processes in a pool can be made to perform some initialization using these arguments. initializer: specify an initialization function for worker processes. initargs : arguments to be passed to initializer function. As a result we map pool object to some square function.As a result, the contents of mylist and definition of square will be distributed among the cores. Once all the worker processes finish their task, a list is returned with a final result.

Multithreading in Python allows multiple threads(smaller units of process) to run concurrently,enabling efficeint multitasking.It is very usefull for I\O bound taks like file handling,network requests,or user interactions.

A process is an executing program with Program code (instruction to run) Data(variables,buffers,workspace) Excecution context(state of the process) buffer - It is temporary storage room used while data is being processed or transferred. This buffer can temporarily hold: Data read from a file Neywork packets User input Workpsace - A workspace is memory used by a program as temporary working area while performing calculations or tasks. Example: Intermediate results memory during computations Temporary arrays memory during sorting

A thread is a smallest unit of execution inside a process. A process can have multiple threads Threads share the same code and global data but have their own registers and local variables(stack). Think of thread as lightweight subprocess A process is managed by a PCB(process control block). Each thread is managed by TCB(Thread control block) and linked to its process. Threads share the process's code and data but threads have their own stacks.

On single-core CPUs, Python achieves the concurrency using context switching This makes threads appear to run in parellel but actually not(multitasking) Multiple tasks help in performing background tasks without blocking the main program. A single thread process execute only one task at a time. A multithreaded process can run multiple tasks in parallel by having separate stacks/registers for each thread, but sharing the same code and data.

Python provides the threading module to work with threads. import threading import time def square(num): print(f"Square: {num*num}") time.sleep(1) def cube(num): print(f"Cube: {num*num*num}") time.sleep(1) t1 = threading.Thread(target=square, args=(4,)) t2 = threading.Thread(target=cube, args=(4,)) t1.start() t2.start() t1.join() #wait for completion t2.join() print("Done!")

The concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor makes it easier to manage multiple threads without manually creating them. from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor def worker(task): print(f"Task {task} running") with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as executor: executor.submit(worker,1) executor.submit(worker,2) Explanation Creates a thread pool with 2 worker threads. Submits two tasks to run in parallel. Each task prints a message (Task 1 running, Task 2 running). The thread pool manages execution and shuts down automatically.

Spawning new threads will becomes problematic if we are dealing with many threads.It is computationally inefficeint to create so many threads which will leads to decline the throughput(efficeincy).An approach to keep up throughput is to create and instantiate a pool of idle threads before and reuse the threads from this pool until all the threads are exhausted. Also the pool keeps track and manages the threads lifecycle and schedules them on the programmers behalf(instead), thus making the code much simpler and less buggy. **Syntax** concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=None,thread_name_prefix="",initializer=None,initargs=()) Parameters max_wokers : It is number of threads (threadpool size),from python 3.8 onwards default value is 32.Out of this 5 threads are preserved for I\O bound task. thread_name_prefix: It is added from 3.6 onwards to give the name of thread for easier debugging purpose. initializer : Initializer takes a callable ,which invoked in the start of every worker thread initargs: It is a tuple of args passed to initializer.

**sumbit(fn,*args,kwargs) It runs a callable or method and returns a Future object representing the execution state of the method. *map(fn,iterable,timeout=None,chunksize=1) It maps the method and iterable together immediately and will raise an exception concurrent.futures.TimeoutError if it fails to do so within the timeout limit. If the itrable is so large, then having chunksize > 1 can improve performance using ProcessPoolExecutor with ThreadPoolExecutor it has no such adavantage ,ie it can aleft to its default value shutdown(wait=True,*,cancel_futures=False) It signals the executor to free up all resources when the future is done executing. It must be called after executor.submit() or executor.map() else it would throw RUntimeError. wait=True makes the method not to return until execution of all threads is done and resource are freed up. cancel_futres=True , then the executor will cancel all the future threads that are yet to start. Example from unittest import result from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor from time import sleep values=[3,4,5,6] def cube(x): print(f"Cube of {x}:{x*x*x}") if __name__=="__main__": with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=5) as executor: executor.submit(cube,2) executor.map(cube,values) Example: Single thread vs MultiThreaded from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor import time from httpx import request import requests img_urls = [ 'https://media.geeksforgeeks.org/wp-content/uploads/20190623210949/download21.jpg', 'https://media.geeksforgeeks.org/wp-content/uploads/20190623211125/d11.jpg', 'https://media.geeksforgeeks.org/wp-content/uploads/20190623211655/d31.jpg', 'https://media.geeksforgeeks.org/wp-content/uploads/20190623212213/d4.jpg', 'https://media.geeksforgeeks.org/wp-content/uploads/20190623212607/d5.jpg', 'https://media.geeksforgeeks.org/wp-content/uploads/20190623235904/d6.jpg', ] t1=time.perf_counter() def download_image(img_url): requests.get(img_url).content print("Downloading----") for img in img_urls: download_image(img) t2=time.perf_counter() print(f"Single Threded:{t2-t1}") print("---------------------------------------------------------") t1=time.perf_counter() def download_image(img_url): requests.get(img_url).content print("Downloading----") with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) as exec: exec.map(download_image,img_urls) t2=time.perf_counter() print(f"Single Threded:{t2-t1}") output Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Single Threded:4.0674544350004 --------------------------------------------------------- Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Downloading---- Single Threded:0.16370929699951375

Thread synchronisation is defined as a mechanism which ensures that two or more concurrent threads do not simultanously execute some particular program segment called critical section Critical section is the part of the program where shared resource is accessed. Concurrent accesses to shared resource can lead to race condition. A race condition occurs when two or more threads can access shared data and they try to change it at the same time.As a result the values of the variables may be unpredictable and vary depending on the timings of context switches of the processes. import threading # global variable x x = 0 def increment(): """ function to increment global variable x """ global x x += 1 def thread_task(): """ task for thread calls increment function 100000 times. """ for _ in range(100000): increment() def main_task(): global x # setting global variable x as 0 x = 0 # creating threads t1 = threading.Thread(target=thread_task) t2 = threading.Thread(target=thread_task) # start threads t1.start() t2.start() # wait until threads finish their job t1.join() t2.join() if __name__ == "__main__": for i in range(10): main_task() print("Iteration {0}: x = {1}".format(i,x)) output Output: Iteration 0: x = 175005 Iteration 1: x = 200000 Iteration 2: x = 200000 Iteration 3: x = 169432 Iteration 4: x = 153316 Iteration 5: x = 200000 Iteration 6: x = 167322 Iteration 7: x = 200000 Iteration 8: x = 169917 Iteration 9: x = 153589 In above program: Two threads t1 and t2 are created in main_task function and global variable x is set to 0. Each thread has a target function thread_task in which increment function is called 100000 times. increment function will increment the global variable x by 1 in each call. The expected final value of x is 200000 but what we get in 10 iterations of main_task function is some different values. This happens due to concurrent access of threads to the shared variable x. This unpredictability in value of x is nothing but race condition note Even with GIL sometimes you need lock, x = 0 Thread A: read x -> 0 (Thread switch) Thread B: read x -> 0 Thread B: write 1 (Thread switch) Thread A: write 1 Expected 2 Actual 1 This is the race condition even with GIL Therefore you still need lock lock = threading.Lock() with lock: x += 1 Then what does the GIL protect? The GIL mainly protects CPython's internal interpreter data structures. Without the GIL, operations like reference counting would require much more synchronization inside the interpreter. The GIL is for interpreter safety, not for ** **.

Threading module provides a lock class to deal with the race conditions. The lock is implemented using the semaphore object provided by OS. A semaphore is a synchronization object that controls access by multiple processes/threads to a common resource in a parallel programming environment. It is simply a value in a designated place in operating system (or kernel) storage that each process/thread can check and then change. Depending on the value that is found, the process/thread can use the resource or will find that it is already in use and must wait for some period before trying again. Semaphores can be binary (0 or 1) or can have additional values. Typically, a process/thread using semaphores checks the value and then, if it using the resource, changes the value to reflect this so that subsequent semaphore users will know to wait. Lock class provides following methods: acquire([blocking]) : To acquire a lock. A lock can be blocking or non-blocking. When invoked with the blocking argument set to True(the default),thread execution is blocked until the lock is unblocked,then lock is set to locked and return True. When invoked with the blocking set to False, thread execution is not blocked. If lock is unlocked,then set it locked and return True else return False immediately. release(): To release a lock. When the lock is locked, reset it to unblocked, and return. If any other threads are blocked waiting for the lock to become unlocked,allow exactly one of them to proceed. if lock is already unlocked, a ThreadError is raised. import threading # global variable x x = 0 def increment(): """ function to increment global variable x """ global x x += 1 def thread_task(lock): """ task for thread calls increment function 100000 times. """ for _ in range(100000): lock.acquire() increment() lock.release() def main_task(): global x # setting global variable x as 0 x = 0 # creating a lock lock = threading.Lock() # creating threads t1 = threading.Thread(target=thread_task, args=(lock,)) t2 = threading.Thread(target=thread_task, args=(lock,)) # start threads t1.start() t2.start() # wait until threads finish their job t1.join() t2.join() if __name__ == "__main__": for i in range(10): main_task() print("Iteration {0}: x = {1}".format(i,x)) output Iteration 0: x = 200000 Iteration 1: x = 200000 Iteration 2: x = 200000 Iteration 3: x = 200000 Iteration 4: x = 200000 Iteration 5: x = 200000 Iteration 6: x = 200000 Iteration 7: x = 200000 Iteration 8: x = 200000 Iteration 9: x = 200000 Adavantages It doesn't block the user.This is because threads are independent of each other. Better use of system resource is possible since threads execute tasks concurrently. Enhanced performance on multi-processor machines. Multi-threaded servers and interactive GUIs use multithreading exclusively. Disadvantages As number of threads increases, complexity increases. Sychronizastion of shared resource is necessary. It is difficult to debug,result is sometimes unpredictable. COnstructing and synchronizing threads is CPU/memory intensive.

When working with multithreading in Pyhton, Lock and RLock are two commonly used synchronization mechanism to ensure mutual exclusion. Mutual exclusion (Mutex): At any given time only one thread is allowed to access a shared resource or execute a critical section of code. Both are part of the threading module ,but behave differently. The key difference between them is Lock can only be acquired once by a thread(A thread cannot acquire the same Lock again while it already holds it.),Whereas Rlock allows the same thread can acquire the thread multiple times. import threading lock=threading.Lock() lock.acquire() print("lock acqiured") lock.acquire() #makes problem lock.release() rlock=threading.RLock() rlock.acquire() rlock.acquire() rlock.release() rlock.release() threading.Lock() initializes the lock, lock.acquire() acquires it and lock.release() releases it for other threads. threading.RLock() initializes an RLock, rlock.acquire() acquires it twice and rlock.release() releases it twice.

A Lock is a synchronization tool provided by the threading module in Python. It is used to ensure only one thread can access a shared resource at a time. Features Mutual Exclusion: Only one thread can hold the lock at a time. Blocking behavior : Threads attempting to acquire a locked lock will wait until its released. No Reentrant : A thread cannot acquire the same lock twice without releasing it,leading to deadlock. import threading lock = threading.Lock() geek = 0 # global variable def fun(value): global geek with lock: geek += value fun(1) fun(2) print(geek) using with lock to ensure mutual exclusion, so only one thread can update geek at a time.

An Rlock is a specialized type of threading module, that allows a thread to acquire the same lock multiple times without causing deadlock. It is designed to solve the problem of reetrancy , Where a thread may need to acquire a lock again while already holding it. Features Reentrant: A thread can acquire the lock multiple times without blocking itself. Acquisation counter: Rlock counts how many times it's acquired by the same thread. Release requirement : The thread must call release() the same number of times it called acquire to fully release lock. import threading geek = 0 rlock = threading.RLock() def fun(): global geek rlock.acquire() # First acquisition geek += 1 rlock.acquire() # Second acquisition geek += 2 rlock.release() # Release first rlock.release() # Release second thread = threading.Thread(target=fun) thread.start() thread.join() print(geek)

Feature Lock Rlock Reacquire Lock Cannot be reacquired by the same thread Can be reacuired multiple times by the same thread Releasing lock Can be releasing by any thread Can only be releasing by acuired thread ownership Owned by one thread at a time Owned by acquiring thread,multiple times Execution speed Faster execution Slower due to tracking acquisition count

Given multiple threads in the program, one thread wants to safely communicate data between them. The safest way to send data from one thread to another is to use Queue from the queue library. TO do this create a thread instance which is shared between threads. Threads then use .put() and .get() operations to add or remove items from the queue. Queue instances already have all of the required locking,so they can be safely shared by as many threads as per requirement. When using queues, it can be tricky for shutdown of the producer and consumer from queue import Queue from threading import Thread # A thread that produces data def producer(out_q): while True: # Produce some data ... out_q.put(data) # A thread that consumes data def consumer(in_q): while True: # Get some data data = in_q.get() # Process the data ... # Create the shared queue and launch both threads q = Queue() t1 = Thread(target = consumer, args =(q, )) t2 = Thread(target = producer, args =(q, )) t1.start() t2.start()

A common solution to this problem is sentinel value. from queue import Queue from threading import Thread # Object that signals shutdown _sentinel = object() # A thread that produces data def producer(out_q): while running: # Produce some data ... out_q.put(data) # Put the sentinel on the queue to indicate completion out_q.put(_sentinel) # A thread that consumes data def consumer(in_q): while True: # Get some data data = in_q.get() # Check for termination if data is _sentinel: in_q.put(_sentinel) break ... The feature of above code is the consumer, upon receiving the sentinel value, immediately places it back onto the queue.This propagates the sentinel to other consumers threads that might be listening on the same queue,Thus shutting them all down one after the other.

The thread communication is one-way process and non-deterministic process.So there is no way to know when receiving threas has actually received a message and worked on it. However Queue objects do provide some basic completion features task_done() and join(). from queue import Queue from threading import Thread # A thread that produces data def producer(out_q): while running: # Produce some data ... out_q.put(data) # A thread that consumes data def consumer(in_q): while True: # Get some data data = in_q.get() # Process the data ... # Indicate completion in_q.task_done() # Create the shared queue and launch both threads q = Queue() t1 = Thread(target = consumer, args =(q, )) t2 = Thread(target = producer, args =(q, )) t1.start() t2.start() # Wait for all produced items to be consumed q.join() Instead of Queue for Thread communication we can build own data structures as long as one adds required locking and synchronization. [ Thread communication -part-2 - pending- geeksforgeeks]

In Python every variable refrence to an objects which is an instance of a class. When an object is declared ,it is assigned to a unique object id. The type of object is declared at runtime and can't be changed afterward.However, for mutable objects- the value or content can change for mutable objects but never the type. Immutable objects​ Once it is created , the value of the object can't be modified. Immutable objects datatypes like int,str,float,bool,unicode and tuple. examples tuple1=(0,2,4,6) tuple1[0]=7 # Throw TypeError: 'tuple' object doesn't support item assigment. message="Hello Python" message[1]=A #Throw TypeError: 'str' object doesn't support item Mutable objects​ Mutable objects datatypes like list,dict,set. Custom classes are mutable - When you create a custom class, python automatically gives for every instances , a private , hidden dictionary called __dict__.This dictionary stores all attributes(variables) belonging to that specific object.Bcz of dictionary is mutable , we can add, delete or change the attributes inside the dictionary at anytime during runtime. List It is mutable , it allows to change the value once it is created. It provide methods like append(),insert(),extend(),remove() and pop(). Operations my_list = [1, 2, 3] my_list.append(4) print(my_list) my_list.insert(1, 5) print(my_list) my_list.remove(2) print(my_list) popped_element = my_list.pop(0) print(my_list) print(popped_element) output [1, 2, 3, 4] [1, 5, 2, 3, 4] [1, 5, 3, 4] [5, 3, 4] 1 Dictionary my_dict = {"name": "Ram", "age": 25} new_dict = my_dict new_dict["age"] = 37 print(my_dict) print(new_dict) output {'name': 'Ram', 'age': 37} {'name': 'Ram', 'age': 37} Set my_set = {1, 2, 3} new_set = my_set new_set.add(4) print(my_set) print(new_set) output {1, 2, 3, 4} {1, 2, 3, 4} MUtable VS Immutable Mutable objects are quicker to access and expensive to change bcz of it involves the creation of the copy.Whereas mutable objects are easy to change Mutable object recommended when there is a change of size or contect of the object. Exception - The immutable objects can contains mutable objects. Under the Hood : id() and hash()​ id() Function id() function returns the actual memory address of an object. If id() stays the same after an operation, the object is mutable. if id() changes, a new object is created(immutable behavior) # Immutable Example (String) name = "Alice" print(id(name)) # Output: 140123456 (example address) name += " Smith" print(id(name)) # Output: 140987654 (The address CHANGED! A new string was made) # Mutable Example (List) numbers = [1, 2, 3] print(id(numbers)) # Output: 140555555 numbers.append(4) print(id(numbers)) # Output: 140555555 (The address is EXACTLY the same) hash() function A hash value is fixed size integer that represents the contents of an object. Only immutable objects can be hashed.Because their hash value remains constant. Mutable objects can't be hashed. if the value of the object can change, its hash would change too. print(hash("Alice")) # Works fine (Immutable) print(hash((1, 2, 3))) # Works fine (Tuple is immutable) # print(hash([1, 2, 3])) # Throws TypeError: unhashable type: 'list' The copy Module: Shallow vs Deep copy When we assign a variable to another(eg : list_b=list_a),python doesn't copy the data. It just creates a pointer to the same exact object in memory. if you change list_b , list_a also changes. To actually dublicate the data , use copy module - which features shallow copy and deep copy. The distinction will be critical for nested structures. Shallow copy (copy.copy())​ A shallow copy creates a new outer object, but popultes it with references to the exact same child objects as the original. if we change the non nested element in the copy, the original is uneffected. if we change the nested element in the copy the original will be affected. Deep copy (copy.deepcopy())​ A deep copy is completely independent.It creates a new outer object and recursively copies all child objects found inside the original.Changes made anywhere in the deep copy not affected in the original. import copy original=[[1,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] shallow_copy=copy.copy(original) deep_copy=copy.deep_copy(original) # The test , modufy a nested element in the shallow copy shallow_copy[0][0]=99 print("Original:",original) # [[99,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] -> CHANGED! print("Shallow:",shallow_copy) # [[99,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] print("Deep:",deep_copy) #[[1,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] -> SAFE! Rule​ Use copy.copy() if your datastructure is entirely flat. Use copy.deepcopy() if you have nested mutable objects and want to ensure 100% independence between the original and the copy.
documents.s: Mutable Vs Immutable objects in Python

documents.t: Once it is created , the value of the object can't be modified. Immutable objects datatypes like int,str,float,bool,unicode and tuple. examples tuple1=(0,2,4,6) tuple1[0]=7 # Throw TypeError: 'tuple' object doesn't support item assigment. message="Hello Python" message[1]=A #Throw TypeError: 'str' object doesn't support item

Mutable objects datatypes like list,dict,set. Custom classes are mutable - When you create a custom class, python automatically gives for every instances , a private , hidden dictionary called __dict__.This dictionary stores all attributes(variables) belonging to that specific object.Bcz of dictionary is mutable , we can add, delete or change the attributes inside the dictionary at anytime during runtime. List It is mutable , it allows to change the value once it is created. It provide methods like append(),insert(),extend(),remove() and pop(). Operations my_list = [1, 2, 3] my_list.append(4) print(my_list) my_list.insert(1, 5) print(my_list) my_list.remove(2) print(my_list) popped_element = my_list.pop(0) print(my_list) print(popped_element) output [1, 2, 3, 4] [1, 5, 2, 3, 4] [1, 5, 3, 4] [5, 3, 4] 1 Dictionary my_dict = {"name": "Ram", "age": 25} new_dict = my_dict new_dict["age"] = 37 print(my_dict) print(new_dict) output {'name': 'Ram', 'age': 37} {'name': 'Ram', 'age': 37} Set my_set = {1, 2, 3} new_set = my_set new_set.add(4) print(my_set) print(new_set) output {1, 2, 3, 4} {1, 2, 3, 4} MUtable VS Immutable Mutable objects are quicker to access and expensive to change bcz of it involves the creation of the copy.Whereas mutable objects are easy to change Mutable object recommended when there is a change of size or contect of the object. Exception - The immutable objects can contains mutable objects.

id() Function id() function returns the actual memory address of an object. If id() stays the same after an operation, the object is mutable. if id() changes, a new object is created(immutable behavior) # Immutable Example (String) name = "Alice" print(id(name)) # Output: 140123456 (example address) name += " Smith" print(id(name)) # Output: 140987654 (The address CHANGED! A new string was made) # Mutable Example (List) numbers = [1, 2, 3] print(id(numbers)) # Output: 140555555 numbers.append(4) print(id(numbers)) # Output: 140555555 (The address is EXACTLY the same) hash() function A hash value is fixed size integer that represents the contents of an object. Only immutable objects can be hashed.Because their hash value remains constant. Mutable objects can't be hashed. if the value of the object can change, its hash would change too. print(hash("Alice")) # Works fine (Immutable) print(hash((1, 2, 3))) # Works fine (Tuple is immutable) # print(hash([1, 2, 3])) # Throws TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
documents.s: Under the Hood : id() and hash()

documents.t: A deep copy is completely independent.It creates a new outer object and recursively copies all child objects found inside the original.Changes made anywhere in the deep copy not affected in the original. import copy original=[[1,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] shallow_copy=copy.copy(original) deep_copy=copy.deep_copy(original) # The test , modufy a nested element in the shallow copy shallow_copy[0][0]=99 print("Original:",original) # [[99,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] -> CHANGED! print("Shallow:",shallow_copy) # [[99,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] print("Deep:",deep_copy) #[[1,3,4,5],[2,5,6,8]] -> SAFE! Rule​ Use copy.copy() if your datastructure is entirely flat. Use copy.deepcopy() if you have nested mutable objects and want to ensure 100% independence between the original and the copy.

OOP is a way of organizing code that uses classes and objects to represent real world entities and their behavior. Object has attribute thing that has specific data and can perform certain actions using methods. Organizes code into object and classes Supports encapsulation which group data and methods together. Enables inheritance for resusability and heirarchy Allows Polymorphism for flexible method implementation Improves modularity, scalabilty and maintainabilty.

Class is the collection of objects. Class defines a set of attributes and methods which the created objects have. Classes are created by keyword class Attributes are the variable belongs to a class. Attributes are always public and can be accessed by using dot(.) operator. class attribute is shared by all instance of the class. __init__() is a constructor method which runs automatically when a new instance is created.It is used to initialize object data. self represents current object allowing each instance to store and access its own data. instance attribute is unique to each objects of the class. class Dog: species = 'Canine' # class attribute def __init__(self,name,age): self.name=name # instance attribute self.age=age # instance attribute

Object is the instance of the class , each object holds its own data.he objects consists of State : It is represented by the attributes and refelects the properties of an object. Behavior : It is represented by the methods of an object, and reflects the response of an object to other objects. Identity : It gives a unique name to an object and enables one object to interact with other objects. # 1. We create the objects my_phone = Smartphone("iPhone 15") abhishek = Person("Abhishek") # 2. The interaction happens abhishek.call_friend(my_phone)

class Dog: species = "Canine" # Class attribute def __init__(self, name, age): self.name = name # Instance attribute self.age = age # Instance attribute # Creating an object of the Dog class dog1 = Dog("Buddy", 3) print(dog1.name) print(dog1.species) sFour Pillars of OOP
Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties and methods of another class.It promotes code reuse and hierarchical classification. TYPES OF DIFFERENT INHERITANCE

A single child inherits from a parent parent class. class Parent: def feature_one(self): return "Feature 1 from Parent" class Child(Parent): def feature_two(self): return "Feature 2 from Child" obj=Child() print(obj.feature_one()) # Feature 1 from Parent ----- Inherited

This is where a child class inherits from more than one Parent class. NOTE:This is superpower unique to C++, Python .Java doesn't allow this because it can cause problem known as Diamond Problem(Child class wouldn't know which parent's method to run if they both had same name).Python solves this using an order system called MRO(Method Resolution Order). class Father: def skills(self): return "Coding" class Mother: def hobbies(self): return "Painting" class Child(Father, Mother): # Inherits from BOTH pass kid = Child() print(kid.skills()) # From Father: Coding print(kid.hobbies()) # From Mother: Painting

A child class inherits from a parent class , which in turn inherits form a grandparent class. class Grandparent: def land(self): return "Owned 100 acres" class Parent(Grandparent): def house(self): return "Built a house on the land" class Child(Parent): def car(self): return "Bought a car to park at the house" kid = Child() print(kid.land()) # Trickled down from Grandparent print(kid.house()) # Trickled down from Parent

One parent class serves as the base for multiple child classes.The child shares the parent features, but they dont share feartures each other. class Vehicle: def info(self): return "This is a transport vehicle." class Car(Vehicle): def speed(self): return "Top speed 120 mph" class Truck(Vehicle): def cargo(self): return "Can carry 5 tons" # Both Car and Truck get 'info()', but Car doesn't get 'cargo()'

Hybrid inheritnce is a combinatin of two or more inheritance listed above. eg: A mix of Multiple and Multilevel inheritance. class School: def school_name(self): return "High School" class Faculty(School): # Hierarchical/Multilevel branch def work(self): return "Teaching" class Sports: # Independent base def activity(self): return "Basketball" class Student(Faculty, Sports): # Multiple Inheritance branch pass

Same operation but different behavior. It allows different classes to have functions or methods with the same name to work differently depending on the type of object they acting upon. Python handles this smoothly because of python in dynamically typed. class Duck: def sound(self): return "Quack" class Dog: def sound(self): return "Woof!" def make_it_speak(animal_obj): animal_obj.sound() make_it_speak(Dog()) # Woof! make_it_speak(Duck()) # Quak! In Pyhton Polymophism is flexible ,usually shows up two main inheritance. With Inheritance and Without Inheritance.

This is a class-object oriented concept.Multiple child class inherits from a parent class, but they override a specific method to change its behaviour. class Document: def __init__(self,name): self.name=name def show(self): pass class WordDocument(Document): def show(self): return f"Opening {self.name} in MIcrosoft Word Document." class PDFDocument(Document): def show(self): return f"Opening {self.name} as PDF layout." def open_file(obj): print(obj.show()) open_file(WordDocument("Resume.docx")) open_file(PDFDocument("Ebook.pdf"))

Python is dynamically typed,Which gives a unique flaovour of inheritance called ** Duck Typing**.The phrase comes from saying :"If it walks like a duck and quacks like duck , its a duck". Python doesn't care about the type of the class or Family tree it only care abouts the object has the method it wants to call. class Duck: def __init__(self,name): self.name=name def sound(self): return f"{self.name} sounds Quack" class Dog: def __init__(self): self.name=name def sound(self): return f"{self.name} sounds Woof" class CarHorn: def __init__(self): self.name=name def sound(self): return f"{self.name} sounds car" # A function doesn't care about What object it gets,as long as it can make sound def make_sound(obj): obj.sound() make_sound(Duck("maju")) mak_sound(Dog("Tilo")) make_sound(CarHorn("i20")) Here Non_animal also works ,beacuse it can make sound. In Many programming language like Java ,if you want a function to accept a parameter , you have to explicitly pass its exact data type or family tree. So in Java was this it wont work for otherthan what type it mentioned in the function.Suppose for this make_sound only accepts object from Animal class , then it wont accept CarHorn class object.

We using polymophism in everyday, without realizing it. Built-in functions like len() change their behavior depending on what you give them. # len() counts characters in a string print(len("Python")) # Output: 6 # len() counts items in a list print(len([1, 2, 3])) # Output: 3 # len() counts key-value pairs in a dictionary print(len({"a": 1, "b": 2})) # Output: 2
documents.s: C. Polymophism in Built-in Python Functions

documents.t: A. Method Overriding​ Redefining a parent class's method inside a child class. B. Method Overloading​ Having multiple functions in the same class with the exact same name but different input arguments.eg: abc(x,y,z) and abc(x,y). NOTE: Python doesn't support native method overloading.If we write 2 functions with the same name , the second one simply overrides the first one.To mimic overloading we use default args (like def add(x,y,z=9) or variable arguments *args).

Python reads code from top to bottom. When it encounters the second function, it simply points the function name to the new definition, completely erasing the first one. Why can't Python do this? Python is a dynamically typed language and functions don't enforce input data types. Furthermore, a Python class is essentially a dictionary under the hood. When you define a method, Python stores it as a key-value pair: {"add": function_object}. If you define a second method named add, Python simply updates that dictionary key to point to the new function, completely erasing the old one. Java, on the other hand, keeps a structured list of distinct method signatures. class Calculator: # First definition (takes 2 arguments) def add(self, x, y): print("Running the 2-argument version...") return x + y # Second definition (takes 3 arguments) # Python looks at this and says: "Oh, 'add' means THIS now!" def add(self, x, y, z): print("Running the 3-argument version...") return x + y + z calc = Calculator() # 1. Testing the 3-argument version (Works perfectly) print(calc.add(5, 10, 20)) # Output: # Running the 3-argument version... # 35 # 2. Testing the 2-argument version (CRASHES!) print(calc.add(5, 10))

In Java, this code works perfectly. Java supports native method overloading because it uniquely identifies a method by its Signature. A method signature in Java isn't just the name of the function; it is a combination of: The method name. The number of parameters. The data types of those parameters. class Calculator { // Method 1: Signature is "add(int, int)" public int add(int x, int y) { System.out.println("Running 2-argument version..."); return x + y; } // Method 2: Signature is "add(int, int, int)" // Java sees this and says: "This is a brand new method!" public int add(int x, int y, int z) { System.out.println("Running 3-argument version..."); return x + y + z; } public static void main(String[] args) { Calculator calc = new Calculator(); // Java checks the inputs: 2 integers -> runs Method 1 System.out.println(calc.add(5, 10)); // Output: // Running 2-argument version... // 15 // Java checks the inputs: 3 integers -> runs Method 2 System.out.println(calc.add(5, 10, 20)); // Output: // Running 3-argument version... // 35 } } a. Default arg​ class Calculator: # We set z to 0 by default. If the user doesn't provide it, it stays 0. def add(self, x, y, z=0): return x + y + z calc = Calculator() # Mimicking 2-argument overloading print(calc.add(5, 10)) # Output: 15 (z automatically becomes 0) # Mimicking 3-argument overloading print(calc.add(5, 10, 20)) # Output: 35 b. Variable args​ class AdvancedCalculator: # *args will capture every single number passed into a tuple named 'args' def add(self, *args): total = 0 for number in args: total += number return total calc = AdvancedCalculator() # You can now call the exact same method with 2, 3, or even 5 numbers! print(calc.add(5, 10)) # Output: 15 print(calc.add(5, 10, 20)) # Output: 35 print(calc.add(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) # Output: 15 c. Overloading Based on Type​ Sometimes in other languages, you overload a function not because of the number of inputs, but because of their type (e.g., wanting add(int, int) to behave differently than add(str, str)). In Python, you can achieve this inside a single function using isinstance() to check the data type: class Greeter: def greet(self, name): # If they pass a list of names if isinstance(name, list): for n in name: print(f"Hello, {n}!") # If they just pass a single string name else: print(f"Hello, {name}!") welcome = Greeter() welcome.greet("Alice") # Output: Hello, Alice! welcome.greet(["Bob", "Charlie"]) # Output: Hello, Bob! \n Hello, Charlie!

Building of data (attributes) and methods (functions) within a class, restricting to access some components to control interactions. In Python, we do this using underscores to signal that a variable is "private." class BankAccount: def __init__(self, owner, balance): self.owner = owner self.__balance = balance # Double underscore makes it private # Getter method to safely look at the balance def get_balance(self): return self.__balance # Setter method to safely change the balance with validation def deposit(self, amount): if amount > 0: self.__balance += amount account = BankAccount("Alice", 1000) # print(account.__balance) # ERROR! Python protects this. account.deposit(500) print(account.get_balance()) # Output: 1500

Abstraction hides the internal implementation details while exposing only the necessary functionality.It focus on "what to do" rather than "how to do". from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class Vehicle(ABC): # Abstract Class @abstractmethod def start_engine(self): pass class Car(Vehicle): def start_engine(self): return "Car engine started. Vroom!" # my_vehicle = Vehicle() # ERROR: You cannot create an instance of an abstract class directly. my_car = Car() print(my_car.start_engine()) # Output: Car engine started. Vroom!

The Diamond Problem occurs when a child class inheritance from two parent classes, And both of those parent classes inherit from the same grandparent class.If both parents override a method from the grandparent, and the child calls that method, which parents method does it use? [ Grandparent (A) ] -> has a method: clean_room() / \ [Parent B] [Parent C] -> both override clean_room() \ / [ Child D ] -> calls clean_room(). Who wins? B or C?

To prevent confusion, every class in Python has an MRO a strict , flat list that tells Python exactly which order to search for a method. Python searches from left to right. we can inspect by using Class.__mro__

How Python builds that MRO without breaking things? It uses an algorithm called C3 Linearization. C3 enforces three strict rules. Preserve Subclass Order : Children are always checked before parents. Preserve Local Precedence : If you write class D(B,C):, B must always checked before C. Monotonicity : If class B comes before class C in an inheritance chain, it must stay that way across the entire application. NOTE : If you try to design a messy inheritance tree that breaks these mathematical rules, Python will refuse to compile it and raise a TypeError: Cannot create a consistent method resolution order (MRO).

Most People thinks super() just calls the parent class.In sigle inheritance it does. But in Multiple Inheritance, super() actually calls the next class in MRO list, not neccessarily the parent. class A: def process(self): print("A's process") class B(A): def process(self): print("Entering B") super().process() # Calls the next thing in the MRO list print("Leaving B") class C(A): def process(self): print("Entering C") super().process() # Calls the next thing in the MRO list print("Leaving C") class D(B, C): def process(self): print("Entering D") super().process() print("Leaving D") # Let's look at D's MRO: D -> B -> C -> A -> object d = D() d.process() The Output Sequence Notice how C gets executed even though B is not a parent of C! Entering D Entering B Entering C A's process Leaving C Leaving B Leaving D

The ABC class is strict structural contract. It allows you to define a template class that can't be instantiated (writing internal logic or code) on its own, forcing any child classes to implement (writing the instruction) specific methods. If you want to be a subclass of AppPlugin, you absolutely must write the your own run() method. from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class AppPlugin(ABC): # Inheriting from ABC makes it abstract @abstractmethod def run(self): """Every child class must implement this method""" pass class MyPlugin(AppPlugin): # If we forget to write run(), Python will crash when we create the object def run(self): return "Plugin is executing!" plugin = MyPlugin() # Works perfectly!

Introduced to make class customisation simpler. __init_subclass__ is method inside the parent class. It triggers automatically when a new child classs inherits from it . It helps parent class to inspect,validate, or register child classes the exact millisecond they are defined, before you ever create an instance. class Registry: plugins=[] def __init_subclass__(cls,**kwargs): super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs) #Automatically keep list of all subclasses created Registry.plugins.append(cls) print(f"Automatically registered plugin: {cls.__name__}") class AudioPlugin(Registry): pass class VideoPlugin(Registry): pass #Output: #Automatically registered plugin: AudioPlugin #Automatically registered plugin: VideoPlugin

class is blueprint of creating an Object, then a Metaclass is a blueprint for creating a Class. In Python, classes themselves are objects. By default, all classes in Python are built by a master metaclass called type. A custom metaclass lets you intercept and completely rewrite how python constructs a class object at runtime. NOTE : You rarely need custom metaclasses. __init_subclass__ handles 95% of use cases cleanly. Use metaclasses only if you are building massive libraries or deep frameworks (like Django or Pydantic) where you must fundamentally alter Python class structures. Metaclass The blueprint that builds classes, giving you complete control over class modification. # A metaclass inherits from 'type' instead of 'object' class UpperCaseAttributesMeta(type): def __new__(mcs, name, bases, class_dict): # We intercept the class creation and force all attributes to uppercase uppercase_dict = {} for key, value in class_dict.items(): if not key.startsWith("__"): # Leave special dunder methods alone uppercase_dict[key.upper()] = value else: uppercase_dict[key] = value # Let 'type' actually construct the class with our modified dictionary return super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, uppercase_dict) # We link our class to the metaclass using the 'metaclass' keyword class User(metaclass=UpperCaseAttributesMeta): role = "admin" status = "active" u = User() # print(u.role) # CRASH! 'role' doesn't exist anymore. print(u.ROLE) # Output: admin (The metaclass rewrote the attribute name!)

The OSI Model (Open Systems Interconnection Model) is a conceptual framework used to understand how different networking systems communicate over a network. Think of it as a 7-layer structure, where each layer has a specific job in sending data from one device to another.
A network = a group of devices connected together. Examples: Your phone + laptop + router Office computers The entire internet

A Local Network is a small network in one place. All devices connected to the same router are part of one local network. Examples: Your home Wi-Fi Office network College lab network Key features of a Local Network: Devices can talk to each other directly Uses: MAC address Local IP (e.g., 192.168.x.x) Controlled by a router

The Internet = a network of networks — a global network connecting many local networks together. It connects: Your home network Other homes Data centers (e.g., Google, Facebook servers) Everything is connected globally. Local Network vs. Internet Local Network Internet Small (home/office) Global Direct communication Through many routers Uses MAC + local IP Uses public IP Controlled by router No single owner

The MAC address (Media Access Control address) is a unique identifier burned into a device's network hardware — like the network card in your laptop or phone It's a hardware identity, not a network identity. Every network-capable device (laptop, phone, router, printer) has one assigned by its manufacturer. In theory: every device in the world has a globally unique MAC address. In practice: what actually matters for communication is uniqueness within your local network.
Closest to the user Provides network services to applications Examples: web browsers, email apps Protocols: HTTP, FTP, SMTP 👉 Example: When you open a website, this layer handles that request.
Responsible for data format, encryption, and compression Converts data into a readable format 👉 Example: Converts data into JSON, encrypts it using SSL.
Manages sessions (connections) between devices Starts, maintains, and ends communication 👉 Example: Keeps your login session active on a website.
Ensures reliable data transfer Handles error detection and flow control Protocols: TCP, UDP 👉 Example: TCP ensures all packets arrive correctly.
Handles routing (path selection) Uses IP addresses to find the destination 👉 Example: Decides how your data travels across the internet.

Responsible for node-to-node delivery Performs error detection at the MAC address level 👉 Example: Communication between two devices in the same network (like Wi-Fi). Data doesn't travel directly from you to a destination (e.g., Google) in one step. It goes step by step, hop by hop — and each step is an example of node-to-node delivery.
Deals with hardware and signals Transmits raw bits (0s and 1s) 👉 Example: Cables, switches, electrical signals.

Layer Action Application You type the message Presentation Message gets encrypted Session Connection is maintained Transport Message is split into packets Network Finds the route Data Link Sent within the local network Physical Sent as electrical signals
documents.s: Simple Real-Life Example: Sending a WhatsApp Message -IMP

documents.t: This section walks through what really happens, layer by layer, when you submit a login form (e.g., on a Django-based website) — from your device, across the internet, to the server, and back.
documents.s: Tracing a Login Request Through the OSI Layers

documents.t: You type your username & password and click Login The browser/app creates a request (usually HTTP/HTTPS)
documents.s: Step 1: You Enter Login Details — Application Layer (7)

documents.t: The data is: Encrypted (HTTPS → SSL/TLS) Formatted (JSON / form data) 👉 Your password is not sent as plain text.
documents.s: Step 2: Data Formatting & Security — Presentation Layer (6)

documents.t: A session is created or reused Maintains the connection between client and server 👉 Example: Keeps track of your login request session.

Uses TCP (reliable) Splits data into packets Adds: Port number (e.g., 443 for HTTPS) Sequence numbers 👉 Ensures all packets reach correctly. Sequnce numbers When TCP splits your data (say, a login request) into multiple packets, each packet doesn't just carry a chunk of your data — it carries a TCP sequence number in its header. Here's what that actually means: It's a byte counter, not a packet counter. TCP doesn't number packets 1, 2, 3... Instead, it numbers the bytes of data. So if your first packet carries bytes 0–999 of your message, its sequence number is 0 (or some random starting value + 0). The next packet, carrying bytes 1000–1999, gets sequence number 1000. And so on. Why bytes and not packets? Because packets can arrive out of order, get duplicated, or get dropped on the internet. A byte-based sequence number lets the receiving side know exactly where each chunk fits into the original message, no matter what order the packets actually arrive in. What it's used for on the receiving end: Reordering — if packet 3 arrives before packet 2, the receiver uses sequence numbers to put the data back in the correct order before handing it to the application. Detecting loss — if the receiver sees sequence numbers 0, 1000, then jumps to 3000, it knows the packet covering 2000–2999 is missing and can ask for it to be resent. Acknowledgment — the receiver sends back an ACK (acknowledgment) referencing the sequence number, confirming "I received up to byte X." This is how TCP guarantees reliable delivery.
documents.s: Step 4: Breaking Data into Packets — Transport Layer (4)

documents.t: Adds: Source IP (your device — your laptop) Destination IP (server) Finds the best path to the server 👉 Like Google Maps for data.
Adds the MAC address Handles communication inside the same local network (Wi-Fi/router)
documents.s: Step 6: Local Delivery — Data Link Layer (2)

documents.t: Converts data into electrical signals / radio waves Sends it through cable/Wi-Fi

The server receives the signal and travels back up: Physical → ... → Application. At the Application Layer, the server: Reads the request Verifies the username & password (database check) Sends a response back down through the layers to you
documents.s: The Reverse Happens on the Server Side

documents.t: Input → HTTP Request → Encryption → Session → TCP Packets → IP Routing → MAC Delivery → Signals → Server → Validate → Response back

OSI Layer Real-World Component Application Django view (/login) Presentation HTTPS (SSL/TLS handled by Nginx) Session Django session / JWT Transport TCP Network IP routing (internet) Data Link MAC (local delivery) Physical Electrical signals / radio waves — Response → Cookie or token

The Session Layer is said to: Start the session Maintain the session End the session 👉 This sounds like "connection management," which can be confusing, because in practice this job is split across two different real components.

✅ Connection management is handled by the Transport Layer (TCP): Keeps the connection alive Handles: Packet order Retransmission Reliability 👉 This is the real "connection manager." ✅ User session tracking is handled by the Application Layer (e.g., Django / backend): Stores login info Uses cookies / session ID 👉 This is user identity tracking, not connection management.

Each device in a local network has both: A MAC address (hardware identity) An IP address (private IP — network identity) 👉 You use the IP address to communicate, but the MAC address is used internally to actually deliver the data. MAC = Media Access Control address Property IP Address MAC Address Type Logical address (assigned by router/network) Physical address (assigned by hardware) Used for Internet routing Local delivery Behavior across hops Stays the same (end-to-end) Changes at each hop Uniqueness Unique per device ✅ (but may be shared publicly via NAT ❌) Always unique ✅, not visible outside local network ❌ Key insight: IP is for decision (routing). MAC is for delivery (local handoff). "Behavior across hops" Imagine your laptop sending a request to Google's server, passing through your router, then your ISP's router, then a few more routers, before reaching Google. IP address — stays the same the whole way: Laptop → Router1 → Router2 → Router3 → Google Source IP: YOUR_PUBLIC_IP (same at every single hop) Dest IP: GOOGLE_IP (same at every single hop) The IP address is the "final destination label" — it doesn't change no matter how many routers the packet passes through. Every router just reads it to decide where to send it next, but never modifies it. MAC address — gets replaced at every single hop: Laptop → Router1: Source MAC = Laptop's MAC, Dest MAC = Router1's MAC Router1 → Router2: Source MAC = Router1's MAC, Dest MAC = Router2's MAC Router2 → Router3: Source MAC = Router2's MAC, Dest MAC = Router3's MAC Router3 → Google server: Source MAC = Router3's MAC, Dest MAC = Google server's MAC This is exactly what you read earlier in your notes: each router strips off the old frame (old MAC addresses) and builds a brand-new frame with new MAC addresses before forwarding to the next hop. MAC addresses only ever describe "the next physical neighbor," never the true original sender or true final destination — that's the IP address's job.
