"Uniqueness" IP address: "Unique per device ✅ (but may be shared publicly via NAT ❌)" Inside your home network, your laptop, phone, and TV each get their own unique private IP (192.168.1.10, 192.168.1.11, etc.) — so far, unique per device, like it should be. BUT once they all go out to the internet, NAT makes them share one single public IP (your router's IP). So from the internet's point of view, your laptop and your phone look like they have the same IP address. That's the "❌" — uniqueness breaks down once you go outside the local network, because multiple real devices end up hiding behind one shared public IP. MAC address: "Always unique ✅, not visible outside local network ❌" The MAC address itself stays truly unique — no sharing, no NAT-style collapsing of multiple devices into one MAC. Your laptop's MAC is always just your laptop's MAC. That's the "✅." BUT the catch is different: it's not that MAC addresses become non-unique — it's that they never even leave your local network in the first place. The moment your data crosses your router, your MAC address gets stripped off and replaced (as shown above). So nobody outside your local network ever even sees your real MAC address to begin with. That's the "❌" — not a uniqueness problem, but a visibility problem.

The problem: Your laptop wants to send data to the internet, but you cannot directly reach every IP on the internet — the internet is made of many networks connected together. Your device is only directly connected to your local network (Wi-Fi/router), so it does not directly know the destination server's MAC address. What actually happens:
documents.s: Why the Data Link Layer Is Needed

documents.t: Your laptop decides: "I need to send this to my router first."

Your device already knows it's router's IP (the default gateway), but not its MAC address: Laptop asks: "Who has this IP (router IP)? Give me your MAC address." Router replies: "Here is my MAC address."
The Data Link Layer creates a frame, adding: Source MAC → your laptop Destination MAC → router

The frame is sent over Wi-Fi to the router. This is the Data Link Layer's job. None of this involves the internet yet. ARP, in particular, only ever works locally — it can't ask "who has this IP" across the internet, only within the same local network. The default gateway is just a technical name for your router's local IP address.
documents.s: Step 4: Send Within the Local Network

documents.t: It looks at the destination IP.

It asks: "Do I know this network directly?" Case 1 — ✅ The destination IP is in the router's local network: Router says: "This is my network." Sends the data directly using the MAC address. Case 2 — ❌ The destination IP is NOT in the local network (the typical case): Router says: "I don't know this directly → send to the next router." It uses its default route: "If I don't know the destination, send everything to the ISP." default route's action is: "if I don't recognize the destination, send it to my ISP.
documents.s: Step 2: Router Checks Its Routing Table

documents.t: Routing decision (Network Layer): Check the routing table using the destination IP. Forwarding (Data Link Layer): Create a new frame using MAC addresses. Destination MAC → next router's MAC address (e.g., ISP router) Source MAC → current router At each router along the path: Removes the previous frame's MAC addresses ❌ Keeps the IP packet intact ✅ Creates a brand-new frame with new MAC addresses ✅ Key takeaway: Decision = Network Layer (IP). Delivery = Data Link Layer (MAC).

At the hardware level (Ethernet/Wi-Fi), devices don't understand IP addresses — they only understand MAC addresses. So at every hop, the router: Looks at the IP address (e.g., the destination server's IP) to decide where to send the data Sends it to the next hop using that hop's MAC address
documents.s: Why Not Just Use IP Directly?

documents.t: 👉 No — previous MAC addresses are not preserved across the journey. Frames (Data Link Layer) are rebuilt at every hop: Each router removes the old frame Keeps the IP packet Creates a new frame with new MAC addresses By the time the request reaches the destination server (e.g., Google): Frame (Data Link Layer): ✅ Destination MAC → Server's MAC ✅ Source MAC → Last router's MAC ❌ NOT your laptop's MAC ❌ NOT any intermediate routers' MACs Packet (Network Layer): ✅ Source IP → Your public IP ✅ Destination IP → Server's IP What is NOT included in the IP packet: ❌ Intermediate router IPs ❌ Path history ❌ Any previous hop's IP

Multiple devices in your home (phone, laptop, TV) all share one public IP address from your router/ISP (e.g., Airtel, Jio). Individually: Laptop ❌ not visible separately on the internet Phone ❌ not visible separately TV ❌ not visible separately 👉 The server only sees the router's single public IP.
This is due to NAT (Network Address Translation). The router: Converts private IP → public IP Keeps track of who sent what

Even though all devices share one public IP, the router uses port numbers to tell them apart. Example — NAT + Port Mapping: Device Private IP Private Port Mapped to (Public) Laptop 192.168.1.10 5001 49.x.x.x:5001 Phone 192.168.1.11 5002 49.x.x.x:5002 Request flow: Laptop sends a request from 192.168.1.10:5001 Router converts it to its public address before forwarding
documents.s: How NAT Still Allows Correct Communication

documents.t: What if two devices use the same private port? Laptop → 192.168.1.10:5001 Tablet → 192.168.1.12:5001 👉 Same port (5001) on both — how does the router tell them apart? First, fix a misunderstanding: the port inside your local network (the private port) does not have to be unique across devices. Multiple devices can use the same private port number.
documents.s: A Tricky Case: Same Private Port on Different Devices

documents.t: The router performs Port Address Translation (PAT): it creates its own unique mapping for each connection, regardless of what private port was used. Real NAT Table: Device Private (IP:Port) Public (IP:Port) Laptop 192.168.1.10:5001 49.x.x.x:6001 Tablet 192.168.1.12:5001 49.x.x.x:6002 👉 Notice: both devices used port 5001 internally ✅, but the router assigned them different public ports ❗

When the server replies, when response reaches to the router ,that time checks its NAT table: 6001 → 192.168.1.10:5001 -> routes the reply to the Laptop ✅ 6002 → 192.168.1.12:5001 -> routes the reply to the Tablet ✅

Private ports can clash, public ports cannot. info Public port cannot reliably or persistently tell which specific device sent them, because ports are temporary and assigned per-connection, not per-device.
Using your public (source) IP, a server can determine general information: 🌍 General Info Country (e.g., India) City (approximate) ISP (e.g., Airtel, Jio) 👉 This is called IP geolocation.
documents.s: What a Server Can Learn From Your IP Address

documents.t: In most home networks, your IP is shared across all devices (phone, laptop, TV) via NAT — they all use the same public IP. So the server sees only one shared IP address, not individual devices. Shared or Unique? Public IP Shared — all your devices (phone, laptop, TV) go out to the internet using the exact same public IP, because they're all behind the same router Public Port Unique for connection — for every active connection, the router assigns a different public port, so each device's traffic can be told apart
documents.s: Important Real-World Case: Shared Home IP

documents.t: if all my devices share one public IP, how does a website still tell my phone apart from my laptop, or tell me apart from someone else on the same network (like at school or office Wi-Fi)? ANS: Public IP tells the server which network the traffic came from (e.g., "this is someone on this specific home/school/office Wi-Fi") Public IP + Public Port is the exact pair the router uses internally (via the NAT table) to know which device sent a specific request This part is accurate, and it's exactly how NAT works.Not the way the server identifying the user.

Where it breaks down for "the server identifying you" The problem is: the public port isn't stable or visible to the server in a way that's useful for identification. The port is temporary, and changes constantly Each new connection (or even a refreshed page) gets a new random public port from the router. Your laptop might use port 6001 for one request, then 6034 for the next, then 6102 for the one after that. There's no fixed "this port = this device" rule that persists. So even if the server logged the port for one request, it's useless for the next request — it'll be a different port. 2. The server is never trying to map ports back to devices — only the router does that The NAT table you learned about is internal to your router, used only so replies get sent back to the right device on your local network. The server on the other end never sees or uses this table — it just sees "a request came from 49.x.x.x:6001" and replies to that same address. The server has no way (and no need) to peek inside your home network and go "ah, port 6001 means it was specifically the laptop." So from the server's point of view, port number ≠ device identity The server could technically notice "two different requests came from two different ports under the same IP," and guess "these are probably two different devices/tabs" — but it has no way to know which device is which, or whether it's the same device opening two tabs versus two separate devices. The port alone carries no identity information to the server — it's meaningful only to the router, transiently, for routing replies. So to directly answer your question: Using IP + port, the server can roughly tell "these requests are probably coming from different connections behind this one IP" — but it cannot reliably or persistently tell which specific device sent them, because ports are temporary and assigned per-connection, not per-device.

Since IP+Port alone can't distinguish between devices/users behind the same NAT, websites use: Cookies — stored in the browser, used to track sessions/logins Login accounts — username/password Device fingerprinting — browser type, OS, screen size, etc.
documents.s: So How Do Websites Identify You Specifically?

documents.t: Device 1 (say, your laptop) at office, IP 192.168.1.10 Device 2 (say, your phone) at office, IP 192.168.1.20 Both go through the same office router, sharing one public IP (say 49.x.x.x) Both log in with the same username/password (let's say it's the same Google account) What happens, step by step Device 1 sends a request Goes out as 192.168.1.10:5001 Router does NAT: assigns public port 6001 Leaves office as 49.x.x.x:6001 Router's NAT table: 6001 ↔ Device 1 (192.168.1.10:5001) Device 2 sends a request (could be at the exact same moment) Goes out as 192.168.1.20:5050 Router does NAT: assigns public port 6002 (a different port — it always picks a free one) Leaves office as 49.x.x.x:6002 Router's NAT table: 6002 ↔ Device 2 (192.168.1.20:5050) Google's server receives both From Google's point of view, it received: Request A from 49.x.x.x:6001 Request B from 49.x.x.x:6002 Same IP, different ports — Google doesn't know or care these are "two different physical devices" — it just sees two separate connections, full stop. 4. Google replies to each, individually Reply for Request A goes back to 49.x.x.x:6001 Reply for Request B goes back to 49.x.x.x:6002 Your office router routes each reply correctly Sees reply addressed to 6001 → NAT table says that's Device 1 → forwards to 192.168.1.10 Sees reply addressed to 6002 → NAT table says that's Device 2 → forwards to 192.168.1.20

One device (your laptop), one private IP: 192.168.1.10 Tab 1: you open Google in one tab Tab 2: you open Google in another tab Same browser, same device, same private IP What happens, step by step Tab 1 makes a request Your OS/browser opens a new connection: 192.168.1.10:5001 Router does NAT, assigns public port 6001 Leaves your office as 49.x.x.x:6001 NAT table: 6001 ↔ 192.168.1.10:5001 (Tab 1's connection) Tab 2 makes a request (even from the exact same device) Your OS picks a different local port for this new connection: 192.168.1.10:5002 Router does NAT, assigns a different public port: 6002 Leaves your office as 49.x.x.x:6002 NAT table: 6002 ↔ 192.168.1.10:5002 (Tab 2's connection) The key thing happening here Even though it's the same device with the same private IP, your operating system assigns a different local port to every separate connection it opens — whether that's two tabs, two apps, or the same app opening two requests at once. This isn't something NAT or the router invents; it's just how TCP connections work in general: every connection gets its own unique combination of (local IP, local port, destination IP, destination port). So: Tab 1's connection: 192.168.1.10:5001 → Google Tab 2's connection: 192.168.1.10:5002 → Google These are two completely separate TCP connections, even though they're from the same device and same browser. Your OS keeps them apart locally, your router keeps them apart via NAT (different public ports), and Google's server keeps them apart as two independent connections too. 3. Google replies to each separately Reply for Tab 1 → goes to 49.x.x.x:6001 Reply for Tab 2 → goes to 49.x.x.x:6002 Router forwards each reply correctly 6001 → maps to 192.168.1.10:5001 → delivered to Tab 1's connection 6002 → maps to 192.168.1.10:5002 → delivered to Tab 2's connection Your OS delivers each reply to the correct tab

Frame (Data Link Layer): the data format used for local delivery. A frame = your data + MAC addresses. Packet (Network Layer): the data format that carries source and destination IP addresses; stays the same end-to-end, while frames are rebuilt at every hop. A Router is a device that connects a local network to other networks (e.g., the internet) — like a bridge or gate between networks. The Local Network includes the router itself, but the router is the special device inside that network that connects it to the outside world. Private IP (Local Network): Used inside your local network Not visible on the internet Each device has a unique private IP and a unique MAC address Public IP (Internet): Given to your router by your ISP Used to communicate on the internet All devices behind the router share this single public IP (via NAT)
Byte code is a low-level intermediate platform indepedent representation of source code. NOTE:Cpython- software (also call- Python interpretor) = Cpython Compiler+PVM (Cpython interpretation part)

1- Compilation - CPython interpreter(Compilor component of the interpreter) automatically transfers your source code (python code) into byte code 2- Caching - To speed up future runs,the byte code is cached in .pyc file within __pycache__ folder. 3- Interpretation - The PVM reads the byte code instructions line by line and executes on the specific os.

Platform indepedent Efficeincy - Byte code is more compact and faster for the PVM to executes more than raw text. Implementation - The byte code format can change b/w python versions. Abstraction - The byte code isn't meant for CPU, it is meant for software based PVM. Internal instructions - The byte code is looks like series of numeric code in PVM Level Representation What it looks like Who reads it Human Code Source Code a+b You (The Coder) Mnemonic Disassembled code BINARY_ADD The dis module Numeric (OPCODE) Actual Byte code 122(example) The PVM Machine level code Binary 01111010 The RAM \ Transistors Mnemonic (pronounced- ne-mon-ik) is simply memory aid.It is a way to replace something complex with something much easier for human to recognize.

We can see the Byte code of any function using built-in dis (disassembler) module. import dis def add(a,b): return a+b dis.dis(add) output​ 4 0 LOAD_FAST 0 (a) 2 LOAD_FAST 1 (b) 4 BINARY_ADD 6 RETURN_VALUE Output Shows the specific operations the PVM performs: LOAD_FAST : Load variable a onto stack LOAD_FAST : Load variable b onto stack BINARY_ADD : Add 2 values. RETURN_VALUE : Return the result.
For portability - python stays flexible as long as the system has a python interpreter, it can run code reagardless of the hardware.

WebRTC is a free, open-source technology that enables real-time communication (audio, video, and data) directly between browsers or devices. In simple terms: It lets two users talk, video call, or share data directly from their browsers — without installing any plugins or third-party apps. Key ideas: Works using JavaScript APIs + HTML5 Enables peer-to-peer (P2P) communication Removes the need for third-party software like Skype (before WebRTC, browsers couldn't natively support audio/video streaming, so dedicated apps like Skype were required) Peer-to-peer (P2P) communication means two devices talk directly to each other, without sending their data through a central server in between. Common misconception: WebRTC is often thought to work only in browsers. While it was originally designed for the web, it has evolved into a universal standard for real-time communication used across almost every platform (mobile apps, IoT devices, desktop software, etc.).

Before WebRTC: Real-time communication required plugins (e.g., Flash) — a plugin was needed to run interactive content on web pages. Needed complex, dedicated server setups to relay audio/video. WebRTC solves this by: Standardizing APIs through W3C and IETF Allowing secure, browser-based communication with no plugins "standardizing" though thses 2 organization, here just means these two organizations wrote down the official rules that browser makers and network engineers had to follow, so that WebRTC would work the same way everywhere

Organization Full Form Role W3C World Wide Web Consortium Standardizes the browser API layer — standardizes web technologies like HTML, CSS, browser behavior, and Web APIs IETF Internet Engineering Task Force Standardizes the network/protocol layer — how the internet communicates underneath The JavaScript API surface is a subset of "Web APIs". IETF has protocols like SDP (Session Description Protocol),ICE,STUN/TURN protocols,DTLS/SRTP.

The W3C published formal specifications defining exactly how JavaScript should be able to talk to a browser's media and networking features. This is what gave us the actual functions developers call, like: WebRTC revolves around 3 core APIs:
Purpose: Captures media from the user's devices Accesses: 🎥 Camera 🎤 Microphone Example use: Capturing local video input for a call

The heart of WebRTC Establishes the connection between two peers Handles: Audio/video streaming Network negotiation Encryption Network negotiation is the process where two peers figure out the best way to actually reach each other over the network.
Used for sending non-media data between peers, such as: Text messages Files Game data

The process begins locally: the app calls getUserMedia() to request access to the camera and microphone. The browser prompts the user for permission. If granted, a MediaStream object is created, containing the audio and video tracks.

Before any media can flow, the two peers must exchange information about their media capabilities (codecs, resolution, IP address, etc.). This exchange uses SDP (Session Description Protocol). The Offer: Peer A creates an "Offer" (an SDP object) and sends it to Peer B through a Signaling Server — a middleman server typically using WebSockets or HTTP. The Answer: Peer B receives the offer, sets it as its "Remote Description," creates an "Answer" (its own SDP), and sends it back to Peer A. Every peer maintains two SDP "slots" in memory: A signaling server is just a regular server (built and hosted by whoever makes the app — not provided by WebRTC itself) that acts as the middleman to introduce two peers to each other before they can connect directly. Slot Meaning Local Description "This is what I am capable of" — my codecs, security keys, IP Remote Description "This is what the other peer is capable of" The "Offer" and "Answer" are both instances of the SDP object. Summary: Devices exchange IP addresses and media capabilities. ⚠️ Important: WebRTC does NOT define how signaling happens. A separate server (e.g., using WebSocket) is required to perform this step.

While signaling is in progress, the peers must find a way to connect their IP addresses directly. This is handled by ICE (Interactive Connectivity Establishment). NAT (Network Address Translation): The technology that lets multiple devices (phone, laptop, smart fridge) share a single public IP address to access the internet. ICE Candidates: Each peer generates a list of potential IP addresses and ports where it can be reached. STUN Server: Tells a device behind a NAT what its public IP address is. TURN Server: If a direct connection isn't possible (e.g., strict corporate firewalls), a TURN server acts as a relay, passing data between the two peers. Firewalls act as the "security guards" of a network, controlling what traffic is allowed in and out. STUN vs TURN​ Server Role Used When STUN Discovers the device's public IP/port Tried first, for direct P2P connection TURN Relays media/data between peers Used only if a direct connection fails ⚠️ Important: WebRTC does not use STUN and TURN simultaneously for data transfer. It always tries STUN first, and falls back to TURN only if needed.

Once SDPs have been exchanged and a valid network path is found via ICE candidates: RTCPeerConnection API establishes the link between peers. The browser performs a security handshake using DTLS/SRTP protcols to ensure all data is encrypted. The peers "poke holes" through their firewalls to allow UDP data packets to flow. DTLS — Datagram Transport Layer Security Think of DTLS as TLS (the same encryption tech behind HTTPS) adapted for UDP instead of TCP. SRTP — Secure Real-time Transport Protocol It's built on top of RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol — the standard for streaming audio/video over a network), adding encryption and authentication. |Protocol|Secures|Used for| |:---|:---|:---| |DTLS|Key exchange + the data channel|Setting up exchanges of encryption keys; encrypting text/files/game data| |SRTP|Audio/video media|Encrypting the actual call media| The problem it solves: By default, most home/office routers and firewalls follow this rule: "Allow outgoing traffic. Block incoming traffic — unless it's a reply to something we already sent out." This is great for security, but it's a problem for P2P: if Peer B tries to send Peer A data out of nowhere, Peer A's firewall sees it as an unsolicited incoming connection and blocks it. How "poking a hole" works: Peer A sends a small outgoing packet toward Peer B (even just a tiny test packet, as part of the ICE connectivity checks). Peer A's own firewall sees this as outgoing traffic it approved, so it temporarily opens up a "hole" — it remembers: "I sent something to Peer B's IP/port, so I'll allow replies back from that same IP/port." Peer B does the same thing in the other direction at the same time — sending its own outgoing packet toward Peer A. Now both firewalls have "holes" open for each other's traffic, because each one thinks it just made an outgoing connection and is only allowing the expected reply. Once both holes are open, real audio/video/data can now flow freely through, in both directions — without either firewall blocking it.

Media tracks (audio/video) now stream directly from one peer to the other. If a Data Channel was created, files or text messages can be sent over the same connection. WebRTC continuously monitors connection quality and can adjust video resolution or bitrate if the network becomes congested.
Signaling → setup only (exchanging connection info) Actual communication → peer-to-peer (direct media/data flow)

Browser asks for camera/microphone permission. Connection info is exchanged with the other user (signaling). Network discovery takes place (ICE/STUN/TURN). A direct connection is established. Video/audio starts streaming instantly.
documents.s: 5. Simple Example: Starting a Video Call - IMP

documents.t: WebRTC powers many apps already in everyday use: Video calls — Google Meet, Zoom Messaging — WhatsApp Web Customer support chat Online gaming Live streaming

Advantages Disadvantages Low latency — direct P2P connection means faster communication Browser compatibility issues — not all browsers fully support all features No plugins — works natively inside the browser High bandwidth usage — especially for video calls Secure — encryption enabled by default Complex setup — requires a signaling server (WebSocket/HTTP) and handling NAT traversal complexity Cost-efficient — less server usage since media flows P2P "High bandwidth usage" means a WebRTC video/audio call consumes a large amount of network data, much more than things like text messaging or even loading a typ…"High bandwidth usage" means a WebRTC video/audio call consumes a large amount of network data, much more than things like text messaging or even loading a typical webpage

Term Definition Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Devices communicate directly with each other, without routing media through a central server Signaling The exchange of connection information (SDP offers/answers, ICE candidates) before communication begins ICE (Interactive Connectivity Establishment) The process of finding the best/most direct network path between peers Codecs Used to compress and decompress audio/video data (e.g., VP8 for video, Opus for audio)

WebRTC is: A modern web standard A technology that enables real-time communication directly in browsers (and increasingly, across other platforms) Built on: JavaScript APIs (getUserMedia, RTCPeerConnection, RTCDataChannel) Peer-to-peer networking (with signaling, ICE, STUN/TURN for connection setup) Built-in encryption (DTLS/SRTP) WebRTC is the backbone of modern real-time web communication apps.

WebSocket API is a browser-side JavaScript API used to open a persistent connection between a client (e.g., a browser) and a server, so both sides can send data to each other without repeatedly making new HTTP requests. It is bidirectional and full-duplex — data can move both ways at the same time, over a single connection.It enables true real time communication. Once the connection is established, the server can push new data to the browser immediately, and the browser can send data back whenever it wants. Important distinction: The WebSocket API is not the same thing as the WebSocket protocol. The protocol defines how client and server communicate at the network level. The API is the JavaScript interface the browser provides for using that protocol (WebSocket, onopen, onmessage, etc.).

WebSocket is built for Client-to-Server communication (like a browser talking to a backend database). WebRTC (Web Real-Time Communication) is built for Peer-to-Peer (P2P) communication (like a browser talking directly to another browser). WebRTC is not a single protocol — it's a framework/set of standards that bundles together multiple existing protocols and APIs to enable real-time peer-to-peer communication. ||WebSocket|WebRTCIs| it one protocol?|✅ Yes — a single protocol (RFC 6455)|❌ No — a framework combining many protocols| Defined by|IETF (single RFC)|W3C (APIs) + IETF (multiple protocols)| Underlying transport|TCP|Typically UDP (via SRTP/DTLS), with ICE/STUN/TURN for connectivity|

Before WebSocket, real-time-ish behavior was faked using: Short polling — the client repeatedly asking the server for updates. Long polling — the client sends a request and the server purposefully holds the connection open, server responding only when new data becomes available, after which the client must immediately send a brand new request to repeat the process(reuest- response cycle). Problems with both: too many requests, high server load, and delays. WebSocket solves this by keeping a single connection open and letting the server send data instantly, without the client asking again and again.

Use the WebSocket API when the application needs instant, two-way communication between browser and server. Typical use cases: Chat systems Video or live interaction apps Multiplayer games Collaborative editing (shared documents) Live dashboards and visualizations Event update systems
Avoid WebSocket when: Data is static Requests happen rarely Keeping a connection open in these cases just wastes server resources.

const socket = new WebSocket("ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws"); socket.onopen = () => { socket.send("Hello server"); }; socket.onmessage = (event) => { console.log("Received:", event.data); }; WebSocket, onopen, and onmessage are all parts of the browser's WebSocket API. const socket = new WebSocket("ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws"); socket.onopen = () => { socket.send("Hello server"); }; socket.onmessage = (event) => { console.log("Received:", event.data); }; WebSocket, onopen, and onmessage are all parts of the browser's WebSocket API.

from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket app = FastAPI() @app.websocket("/ws") async def websocket_endpoint(webs: WebSocket): await webs.accept() while True: raw_text = await webs.receive_text() await webs.send_text(f'received data {raw_text}') # To test: ws://localhost:8000/ws (e.g., in Postman) This creates a WebSocket endpoint at /ws, accepts the incoming connection, waits for text from the client, and echoes it back — a tiny echo server. Note on terminology: In frontend code, WebSocket refers to the browser's WebSocket API. In FastAPI backend code, WebSocket refers to the backend framework's WebSocket object/class. Same name, different layer.
Protocol Behavior HTTP request → response WebSocket open connection → both sides keep talking live

The two pieces WebRTC — carries the actual audio/video Once two browsers are connected, the actual video and audio stream flows directly between them (peer-to-peer), not through your server. Runs mostly over UDP, optimized for low latency — a bit of dropped video data is fine; nobody wants delay. Handles the media capture (camera/mic), encoding, and the direct peer connection. WebSocket — handles signaling (the "let's connect" handshake) Before two browsers can talk directly via WebRTC, they need to exchange some setup information: "here's my video format," "here's my network address," "I want to call you." This exchange is called signaling, and WebRTC does not define how to do it — it's left up to the developer. WebSocket is a common (but not the only) way to implement signaling, because it's a persistent connection that lets your server relay these setup messages between the two browsers in real time.
documents.s: Video calling in browser - WebRTC and WebSocket

documents.t: HTTP is a request → response protocol. Client → Request → Server Client ← Response ← Server ❌ Connection usually ends Key ideas: Client always starts the exchange. Server only replies. No continuous communication. Server cannot push data to the client without a prior request (without tricks like polling). This is why HTTP is called unidirectional (one-way per request).

WebSocket is a persistent, two-way communication channel. Client ↔ Server (both can send anytime) ✅ Connection stays open Key ideas: After the connection opens, both sides can talk freely. No need to send a new request every time. This is why WebSocket is called full-duplex.

Aspect HTTP WebSocket Communication style Client → Server only; server can't push without a request Client ↔ Server, both directions, anytime Connection behavior Opens → responds → closes Opens once → stays open Overhead Sends full headers every request (heavy) Small frames after handshake (lightweight) Latency Slower — new request/response cycle each time Faster — no repeated setup Scalability Easier (stateless) — supports vertical & horizontal scaling well Harder (stateful) — needs sticky sessions or shared state (e.g., Redis) for horizontal scaling Typical use cases Static pages, normal APIs, file downloads, one-time data Chat apps, live notifications, online games, stock updates

WebSocket connections start as HTTP and then upgrade: HTTP request → Upgrade → WebSocket connection So they are different protocols, but WebSocket uses HTTP to start the connection. They are not competitors — WebSocket is not a replacement for HTTP/REST; modern apps often use both together (REST for normal data, WebSocket for real-time features).
HTTP = ask and get once. WebSocket = stay connected and keep talking.
Yes — WebSocket runs on top of TCP, not UDP.

1. Starts with HTTP (over TCP): Client sends: Upgrade: websocket Server responds: HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols This handshake happens entirely over TCP. 2. After the upgrade: The connection becomes a WebSocket connection. It still uses the same underlying TCP connection — no new connection is opened. WebSocket = HTTP upgrade + persistent TCP connection
WebSocket needs: ✅ Reliable delivery ✅ Ordered messages ✅ No data loss TCP provides exactly this.
Scheme Port http:// 80 ws:// 80 https:// 443 wss:// (secure WebSocket) 443 WebSocket runs on top of existing HTTP infrastructure, which is why it reuses HTTP's default ports.

WebSocket WebRTC Protocol TCP UDP (mostly) Speed Moderate Very fast Reliability High Lower (but real-time-friendly) Use case Chat, notifications Video/audio calls Analogy: WebSocket (TCP) = sending messages with confirmation 📩 WebRTC (UDP) = a live conversation 🎤

No — WebSocket is a protocol, not just a browser feature. It's a common misunderstanding that WebSockets are web-only, because: They started in browsers (JavaScript). They're heavily used in web apps (chat, live updates). Reality: any application that can open a TCP connection and speak the WebSocket protocol can use WebSockets.
documents.s: 4. Is WebSocket Only Used on the Web?

documents.t: Web browsers — chat apps, notifications, live dashboards. Mobile apps (Android/iOS) — real-time messaging, ride-tracking apps (e.g., Uber-style live location). Backend services — microservice communication, real-time data pipelines. Example libraries: Python (asyncio, websockets), Node.js (ws, socket.io). Games — multiplayer games, live player position updates. IoT devices — smart devices and sensors streaming live data.

WebSocket reduces overhead by exchanging mostly just message data — not exactly "only" message data, but very close to it, with minimal extra framing overhead compared to HTTP's full headers on every request fpor message data. Http has more overhead for sending message data.
documents.s: WebSocket's framing overhead compared to HTTP

documents.t: Technically yes, but it's not what it's designed or used for. WebSocket can send two kinds of data: Text frames (usually UTF-8 / JSON) Binary frames (raw bytes — could be an image, a chunk of audio, anything) So in theory, you could chop up audio/video into binary chunks and send them frame-by-frame over WebSocket. Some applications do use WebSocket for low-volume audio/video-adjacent tasks (like sending periodic snapshots, or low-bitrate audio clips). But for real-time, continuous audio/video calls, WebSocket is a poor fit for audio/video,becasue |Issue|Why it hurts video/audio| |Runs over TCP|TCP guarantees delivery and order — so if one packet is lost, it waits and retransmits before delivering anything newer. For live video, a late frame is useless; you'd rather drop it and move on.| |No built-in media optimization|No native support for things like jitter buffering, codec negotiation, or adaptive bitrate| |Goes through a server|All data is relayed via your server — adds latency and bandwidth cost compared to direct peer-to-peer| That's exactly why WebRTC exists — it uses UDP (via SRTP), which tolerates dropped packets gracefully (a skipped video frame is fine — better than freezing to wait for it), and it connects peer-to-peer, skipping the server entirely for media.

REST API — uses the HTTP request–response model: client sends a request, server sends a response, connection ends. WebSocket API — uses a persistent, full-duplex connection: one connection stays open, and both client and server can send data anytime.
REST: Client → Request → Server → Response → Done (one-time communication) WebSocket: Client ↔ Server (continuous) (real-time communication)

Feature REST API WebSocket API State Stateless (no stored data) Stateful (stores connection data) Direction Unidirectional (either server or client per request) Bidirectional Model Request–response Full-duplex Headers/Overhead HTTP headers sent with every request No repeated header overhead once connected Connection New TCP connection per request Single persistent connection What identifies a "request" HTTP Method + path, fresh each time The connection itself, identified by client IP/port, stays the same for the whole session Speed Slower Faster Buffering No memory/buffer needed to hold data Needs memory/buffers to manage live data Scaling Supports vertical and horizontal scaling easily Mainly easy for vertical scaling; horizontal scaling is harder(bcz of stateful)

REST API ✅ Advantages: Simplicity, flexibility, caching, statelessness. ❌ Disadvantages: Limited real-time capability; performance overhead since each request/response must carry all necessary info; can get complex for large apps. WebSocket API ✅ Advantages: Real-time communication, efficient (one persistent bidirectional connection), scalable (one server can support thousands of websocket connections, bcz of websocket is lightweight), low overhead (single connection for ongoing transmission). ❌ Disadvantages: More complex to design and implement than REST; not supported in all environments; vulnerable to security issues if not properly secured. Similarities: Both are used for web applications between client and server, can be customized, and can be secured using authentication and encryption.

Best for REST Best for WebSocket Fetching data occasionally Real-time apps CRUD operations (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) Continuous updates User data, product lists Chat apps, live stock prices, online games
REST → like sending an email 📧: send → wait → receive → done. WebSocket → like a phone call 📞: stay connected, talk anytime.
REST → like sending an email 📧: send → wait → receive → done. WebSocket → like a phone call 📞: stay connected, talk anytime.

WebSocket is not a replacement for REST: REST = simple, scalable. WebSocket = fast, real-time. Modern applications often use both together — REST for standard data operations and WebSocket for real-time features. Final answer: REST API uses a request–response model over HTTP, where each interaction is independent, while WebSocket API maintains a persistent connection allowing real-time, two-way communication between client and server.
Increasing the power of one server (more CPU, more RAM): 1 server → stronger server

Adding more servers, distributing load through a load balancer. Users ↓ Load Balancer ↙ ↓ ↘ Server1 Server2 Server3 Example: 1,000 users on 1 server would overload it. Spread across 5 servers, each handles ~200 users — faster and more stable. Common load balancers: Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ELB/ALB.

HTTP WebSocket Vertical scaling ✅ Supported ✅ Supported Horizontal scaling ✅ Easy ⚠️ Harder Why Stateless — each request is independent, so a load balancer can send any request to any server Stateful — the connection stays open and the server "remembers" the client, requiring sticky sessions or a shared system (e.g., Redis, message broker) to coordinate across servers

WebSocket — client ↔ server communication. Goes through a server, persistent connection, used for general real-time apps. WebRTC — peer-to-peer (P2P) communication. Direct client ↔ client; the server is only used for initial setup (signaling). Designed for media (audio/video).

Aspect WebSocket WebRTC Architecture Client ↔ Server ↔ Client (all data goes through the server) Client ↔ Client (direct; server not used after setup) Protocol TCP UDP (mostly) Latency Low Ultra-low (no middle server, uses UDP) Data type Any — text, JSON, binary Optimized for audio, video, live streaming Reliability Reliable (TCP guarantees delivery) May drop packets (UDP) — acceptable for live video/audio Scalability(server load grows as more users join) Server handles all connections; more users = more server load (one persistent connection per client) Less server load (P2P), but hard to scale to large groups since many direct client-to-client connections are needed Setup complexity Simple Complex — needs ICE candidates, STUN/TURN servers

Not correct. UDP is connectionless. WebRTC is not connectionless — even though it uses UDP, it builds a connection-like system on top of it. WebRTC adds, on top of raw UDP: Connection setup (ICE) Session management Encryption (DTLS) Optional reliability for data channels WebRTC still needs servers for connection setup (signaling) and NAT traversal (STUN/TURN). After that, communication is direct.
documents.s: Common Misconception: "WebRTC Is Connectionless Because It Uses UDP"

documents.t: Use WebSocket for Use WebRTC for Chat apps 💬 Video calls 📹 Notifications 🔔 Voice calls 🎧 Real-time dashboards 📊 Screen sharing 🖥️

WebSocket WebRTC Communication Client–Server Peer-to-Peer Protocol TCP UDP Latency Low Very low Best for Data Audio/Video Complexity Easy Complex Final answer: WebSocket is used for real-time communication between client and server over TCP, while WebRTC enables direct peer-to-peer communication (usually over UDP), making it ideal for ultra-low-latency applications like video and voice calls.

Client sends an HTTP request requesting an upgrade: GET /ws HTTP/1.1 Upgrade: websocket Connection: Upgrade This is not for sending data — it's asking, "Can we switch to WebSocket?" Server responds: HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols This means "Yes, switching to WebSocket." The connection becomes persistent. HTTP is no longer used for messages — both sides can now send data anytime, with no more request–response cycles.

Even though the handshake itself is an HTTP request, using ws:// tells the browser: "Start a WebSocket connection (not a normal HTTP request)." This matters because the browser needs to: Add the special Upgrade and Connection headers. Keep the connection open. Switch into the WebSocket protocol after the handshake completes. This only happens automatically when using the WebSocket() constructor with a ws:// or wss:// URL.
HTTP is stateless — each request is handled independently. WebSocket is stateful — the connection between client and server stays alive until either side terminates it.

When you create a connection: const socket = new WebSocket("ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws"); You can listen for these core lifecycle events in Browser API: Event Fires when... Notes open Connection is successfully established (after handshake) Start of communication message ⭐ Server sends data to the client Most-used event — this is how you receive updates close Connection is closed Provides event.code (why it closed) and event.reason (optional message) error Something goes wrong (server unreachable, network issue, protocol error) Useful for debugging

// Using addEventListener socket.addEventListener("open", () => { console.log("Connected"); }); socket.addEventListener("message", (event) => { console.log(event.data); }); // Using direct properties (same behavior) socket.onopen = () => { console.log("Connected"); }; socket.onmessage = (event) => { console.log(event.data); }; Summary: open → connection ready, start sending. message → main application logic (receiving data). close → cleanup or reconnect logic. error → debugging. Rule: You can only call socket.send() after the open event has fired.

FastAPI provides a dedicated decorator for WebSocket routes: @app.websocket("/ws") async def websocket_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket): await websocket.accept() while True: data = await websocket.receive_text() await websocket.send_text(data) @app.websocket("/ws") defines a WebSocket endpoint, not a normal HTTP API route. await websocket.accept() is the most important line — it accepts the connection. Without it, the connection fails. The while True loop keeps the connection alive: wait for a message → receive it → send a response.

const socket = new WebSocket("ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws"); socket.addEventListener("open", () => { console.log("Connected"); }); socket.send("Hello"); socket.onmessage = (event) => { console.log(event.data); };

WebSocket communication needs continuous waiting and sending. FastAPI uses async/await so the server is non-blocking — it can handle multiple users concurrently without separate threads per connection.
Chat apps, live notifications, stock price updates, multiplayer games — anywhere the server needs to push data without waiting for a request.
A real-time chat app where: Multiple users connect. Each user sends messages. The server distributes (broadcasts) messages to other connected users.
Browser (User A) ─┐ ├──> FastAPI Server ───> All Users Browser (User B) ─┘ The server acts as a middleman that receives and distributes messages.

// 1. Create connection with a unique user ID const socket = new WebSocket('ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws/' + id); // 2. On connect socket.addEventListener('open', () => { showMessage('Connected to server.'); }); // 3. Sending a message socket.send(inputValue); // 4. Receiving a message socket.onmessage = (event) => { showMessage(event.data); };

This class tracks all connected clients: class ConnectionManager: def __init__(self): self.active_connections: List[WebSocket] = [] async def connect(self, websocket: WebSocket): await websocket.accept() self.active_connections.append(websocket) def disconnect(self, websocket: WebSocket): self.active_connections.remove(websocket) async def send_personal_message(self, message: str, websocket: WebSocket): await websocket.send_text(message) async def broadcast(self, message: str, websocket: WebSocket): for connection in self.active_connections: if connection == websocket: continue await connection.send_text(message) Method Purpose connect() Accepts the connection and adds the user to the active list disconnect() Removes a user when they leave send_personal_message() Sends a message to one specific user broadcast() Sends a message to all other connected users (skips the sender, since the sender already got their own copy)

connectionmanager = ConnectionManager() @app.websocket("/ws/{client_id}") async def websocket_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket, client_id: int): await connectionmanager.connect(websocket) try: while True: data = await websocket.receive_text() # Send back to the same user await connectionmanager.send_personal_message(f"You : {data}", websocket) # Broadcast to everyone else await connectionmanager.broadcast(f"Client #{client_id}: {data}", websocket) except WebSocketDisconnect: connectionmanager.disconnect(websocket) await connectionmanager.broadcast(f"Client #{client_id} left", websocket) WebSocketDisconnect is the exception raised when a client disconnects (tab closed, network lost, etc.). On disconnect, the user is removed from the connection list and others are notified.

Step What happens Method/event 1. User opens the page ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws/ Browser creates the WebSocket and starts the handshake , and the hanshake request (HTTP upgarde) is send to server new WebSocket(...) triggers it 2. Server accepts it Backend explicitly accepts the connection in hadshake await websocket.accept() 3. Handshake completes Browser confirms the connection is now open socket.onopen fires (frontend) 4. User sends a message Frontend sends data over the open socket socket.send(...) 5. Server receives it Backend's loop picks up the incoming text data = await websocket.receive_text() 6. Server sends it back to sender + broadcasts to others Two separate sends from the backend send_personal_message() → websocket.send_text(...); broadcast() → connection.send_text(...) for each other client 7. Repeat indefinitely The while True loop keeps listening for more messages —

Persistent connection — one connection handles many messages; no repeated HTTP requests. Connection list (self.active_connections) — how the server tracks who's currently connected. Broadcast logic — the core mechanism behind chat apps. Infinite loop (while True) — keeps the connection alive.

This example stores active_connections in memory, which means: ❌ Not scalable — it only works on a single server instance. Real-world apps typically use Redis, message queues, or pub/sub systems to share connection state across multiple server instances. One-line summary: FastAPI chat app = WebSocket connections + a connection list + broadcast messages. Where is the Websocket()? A browser-based frontend app (React, Vue, plain JS in an HTML page) runs inside the browser, and when it(browser) calls new WebSocket(...), it's using the browser's built-in WebSocket API.
documents.s: Limitation of example and Real-World Improvement

documents.t: Who/what Role Browser Provides the WebSocket class as part of its built-in JavaScript environment (like it also provides fetch, document, console, etc.) Your code Writes new WebSocket(...) — this is your instruction, executed by the browser's JS engine Browser (again, after your call) Once your code creates the object, the browser's internals take over: it sends the actual handshake request over the network, manages the TCP connection, parses incoming frames, and fires events back to your code (onopen, onmessage, etc.)

Plain Django (HTTP) follows: client → request → server → response → connection closed. The server cannot send data anytime — it can only reply when asked. With WebSockets: client ⇄ server, connection stays open, enabling real-time communication where the server can push data anytime. Useful for chat apps, live notifications, dashboards.
Django, by default, uses WSGI (synchronous) — which only supports HTTP. To add WebSocket support, Django needs Django Channels, which switches to ASGI (asynchronous).
documents.s: Why Plain Django Can't Do WebSockets

documents.t: Browser (WebSocket) ↓ ASGI Server (Daphne) - started via `daphne project.asgi:application`, which loads the `application` object from asgi.py ↓ Django Channels ↓ Consumer (the WebSocket equivalent of a view)

1. Install packages pip install channels daphne channels → adds WebSocket support. daphne → an ASGI server (like Gunicorn, but async-capable). 2. settings.py INSTALLED_APPS = [ ..., 'channels', ] ASGI_APPLICATION = 'project.asgi.application' This tells Django to use ASGI instead of WSGI. 3. ASGI configuration (asgi.py) — the core routing decision-maker: application = ProtocolTypeRouter({ "http": get_asgi_application(), "websocket": URLRouter( chat.routing.websocket_urlpatterns ), }) Request type Routed to HTTP Django views WebSocket Consumer (the WebSocket "view") 4. Consumer — the most important part A Consumer is the WebSocket equivalent of a Django view. from channels.generic.websocket import WebsocketConsumer class ChatConsumer(WebsocketConsumer): def connect(self): self.accept() def receive(self, text_data): data = json.loads(text_data) message = data['message'] self.send(text_data=json.dumps({'message': message})) Django HTTP Django Channels Consumer request receive() response send() Django Channels: receive() and send() Concept What it is receive() The method that's called with incoming data from the client — this is how data arrives, same role as request send() The method you call to send data back to the client — this is how data leaves, same role as response 5. Routing (routing.py) websocket_urlpatterns = [ path('ws/chat/', ChatConsumer.as_asgi()), ] 6. Frontend (JavaScript) const socket = new WebSocket("ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws/chat/"); socket.onmessage = function(e) { const data = JSON.parse(e.data); }; socket.send(JSON.stringify({ 'message': message })); 7. Run the server daphne project.asgi:application Important: Django's default runserver (WSGI) is not enough — an ASGI server like Daphne is required. daphne project.asgi:application └──┬──┘ └─────┬─────┘ module path the variable name (project/asgi.py) (application = ProtocolTypeRouter({...}))

Browser opens ws://127.0.0.1:8000/ws/chat/. here new WebSocket() will trigger and send hanshake request to server. Request goes to: ASGI → ProtocolTypeRouter → "websocket" key in ProtocolTypeRouter. Routed to ChatConsumer. Lifecycle events map to consumer methods: first accept event happens to the HTTT handshake request.Then other events . | Event | Consumer method | |---|---| | Connect | connect() | | receives data from the client | receive() | | Send response to client | send() |

WebSocket = persistent connection — no request–response cycle. Consumer = WebSocket handler, analogous to a Django view. Channels = the bridge that gives Django WebSocket support. ASGI vs WSGI: | | WSGI | ASGI | |---|---|---| | Sync | ✅ | ❌ | | Async | ❌ | ✅ | | WebSockets | ❌ | ✅ |
Production chat systems built this way typically add: chat rooms (multiple users), groups (broadcast messaging), and Redis (for scaling across servers).
Daphne is an ASGI server — it runs your Django app and knows how to handle WebSockets, HTTP, and async connections. It's the program that sits between the client and your Django (ASGI) application.

Normal Django (WSGI): Browser → Gunicorn → Django (WSGI) → Response ❌ Only HTTP, no WebSockets. With Channels + Daphne (ASGI): Browser → Daphne → Django (ASGI) → Consumer/View ✅ Handles HTTP, WebSockets, and async tasks.

Accepts the connection — either HTTP or WebSocket. Understands the protocol type: Protocol Routed to HTTP Django views WebSocket Consumer Keeps the connection open for WebSockets — the key difference from a traditional WSGI server: Gunicorn Daphne Request → Response → Close Connect → Stay open → Send/Receive anytime (onlyu for websocket)

Inside asgi.py: application = ProtocolTypeRouter({ "http": get_asgi_application(), "websocket": URLRouter( websocket_urlpatterns ), }) It distinguishes HTTP vs WebSocket by inspecting the incoming request: HTTP request: GET /home HTTP/1.1 → Routed to "http". WebSocket request: GET /ws/chat HTTP/1.1 Upgrade: websocket Connection: Upgrade The special Upgrade: websocket header signals → routed to "websocket".

Case 1 — HTTP request: Browser → Daphne → asgi.py → ProtocolTypeRouter → "http" → get_asgi_application() → Django URL routing → views.py Case 2 — WebSocket request: Browser → Daphne → asgi.py → ProtocolTypeRouter → "websocket" → URLRouter → websocket_urlpatterns → Consumer HTTP WebSocket Routing file urls.py routing.py

def receive(self, text_data): data = json.loads(text_data) message = data['message'] receive() is automatically called by the framework whenever a message arrives. text_data is passed in for you; you manually parse the JSON. Mental model: "When a message comes in → run this function."

data = await websocket.receive_text() You explicitly call receive_text(). It asynchronously waits for a message and returns the text directly. Mental model: "Keep listening and fetch messages yourself."
Django Channels FastAPI Style Event-based Async loop Receiving data Framework auto-calls receive() You call receive_text() Control Framework-driven Developer-driven

Django Channels is built on an event system that handles the lifecycle automatically: connect(), receive(), disconnect(). FastAPI is built on explicit async/await control — you manage the loop, receiving, and sending yourself.

Django: receive(self, text_data) only handles text frames by default. Binary data arrives via a separate bytes_data parameter. FastAPI: you explicitly choose what to receive — await websocket.receive_text() or await websocket.receive_bytes().
info This topic is important.
documents.s: 15. Sync vs Async in Django Channels -

documents.t: Because they use a synchronous consumer: class ChatConsumer(WebsocketConsumer): def receive(self, text_data): self.send(text_data=text_data) No async, no await — it runs in a thread (blocking style).
documents.s: Why Django Examples Often Skip async/await

documents.t: 1. Sync Consumer from channels.generic.websocket import WebsocketConsumer class ChatConsumer(WebsocketConsumer): def receive(self, text_data): self.send(text_data=text_data) 2. Async Consumer from channels.generic.websocket import AsyncWebsocketConsumer class ChatConsumer(AsyncWebsocketConsumer): async def receive(self, text_data): await self.send(text_data=text_data)
documents.s: Django Channels Supports Two Consumer Types

documents.t: Simplicity — easier to understand, no need to think about async yet. Compatibility — Django's ORM is mostly synchronous (User.objects.all()), which works easily in a sync consumer.
documents.s: Why Tutorials Often Use the Sync Version

documents.t: FastAPI is built on ASGI with an async-first design — there is no synchronous option, unlike Django. async def endpoint(): await something()
Django Channels FastAPI Sync support ✅ ❌ Async support ✅ ✅ Default style Sync Async

If using AsyncWebsocketConsumer, never run blocking ORM calls directly: # ❌ BAD — blocks the event loop User.objects.all() Instead, wrap blocking calls: # ✅ GOOD from asgiref.sync import sync_to_async users = await sync_to_async(User.objects.all)()

Use AsyncWebsocketConsumer when you have: Many concurrent users (e.g., chat apps) Real-time updates External API calls Non-blocking task requirements One-line clarity: FastAPI = "everything async." Django = "you choose sync or async."
info This topic is imp
documents.s: 16. Async Database Access in Django and FastAPI

documents.t: Django's ORM is synchronous (blocking). A call like: User.objects.all() blocks the thread until the database responds. You cannot simply write: await User.objects.all() # ❌ ERROR — not awaitable because it's not an async function and doesn't return a coroutine. What actually happens during a sync query: Python → DB driver → Database → wait → response → return During this wait, the thread is blocked and can't handle other requests.
documents.s: Why DB Queries Aren't Naturally await-able in Django

documents.t: from asgiref.sync import sync_to_async users = await sync_to_async(User.objects.all)() Internally, this moves the synchronous call to a thread pool, runs the blocking DB query there, and returns the result without blocking the main async event loop. The async app itself doesn't block — but the DB call is still sync internally. Your async event loop (main thread) ↓ sync_to_async grabs a separate thread from a thread pool ↓ Runs User.objects.all() on THAT thread ← still fully synchronous/blocking, right here ↓ That thread blocks while waiting for the DB ↓ Once it's done, the result is handed back to your async event loop ↓ Your main event loop was free to do other things the whole time Term Meaning-"Doesn't block" (the async app)-Your main event loop stays free to handle other tasks while the DB call happens elsewhere. Term Meaning"Sync internally" (the DB call)- The actual database query is still a normal blocking call — it just happens on a separate worker thread instead of the main thread The blocking is real — it(sync_to_async) didn't get magically removed. It just got moved to a thread that isn't doing anything else important, so its blockage doesn't stall the rest of your app.
documents.s: Using the ORM From Async Code

documents.t: Database drivers — most traditional drivers (e.g., psycopg2 for PostgreSQL) are blocking/sync by design. (A database driver is a library that lets your Python code talk to a database.) Framework design — Django was built before async became popular, and its entire ORM is designed around synchronous execution.

FastAPI can do fully async DB access using specialized async-native libraries: Library What it actually is Talks directly to the database? asyncpg A true DB driver — written specifically for async PostgreSQL access ✅ Yes, directly asyncmy A true DB driver — async MySQL driver ✅ Yes, directly databases A query library / thin wrapper — not a driver itself ❌ No — it sits on top of drivers like asyncpg SQLAlchemy (async mode) A full ORM — also not a driver itself ❌ No — it also relies on an underlying driver to actually talk to the DB These are: ✅ Fully asynchronous ✅ Work with async/await ✅ Non-blocking ✅ Designed for high concurrency SQLAlchemy is a hybrid ORM — originally synchronous, with async support added in version 1.4+. result = await database.fetch_all(query) This is truly non-blocking, using an async DB driver.
Django ORM Async-native (e.g., SQLAlchemy async, asyncpg) Async support ❌ ✅ Blocking ✅ ❌ Needs wrapper (sync_to_async) ✅ No

class ChatConsumer(AsyncWebsocketConsumer): async def receive(self, text_data): users = User.objects.all() # ❌ BAD — blocks the event loop, kills performance Correct approach: from asgiref.sync import sync_to_async users = await sync_to_async(User.objects.all)()

If the app is mostly DB-heavy with standard CRUD operations(std crud operations mostly have noraml traffic), a sync Django setup is often simpler and perfectly adequate — async only pays off under high concurrency or many simultaneous long-lived connections. Async doesn't make a single DB-heavy query run faster — the database still takes the same time to do its work. Async's benefit is letting your app stay responsive to other requests while waiting on that DB time. So "DB-heavy but normal traffic" doesn't automatically benefit from async — only "DB-heavy and lots of simultaneous waiting requests" does.ASync helps if there simultaneous waiting requests case , not with normal traffic for "DB heavy " operations.
documents.s: When Sync Django Is Actually Fine

documents.t: Authentication answers: "How does the server know who you are?" (e.g., after you log in, the server must remember you for future requests.) There are two major approaches:

Idea: The server stores your login info; the browser only holds a reference to it. Flow: Login: username + password → server. Server creates a session: session_id = "abc123", stored server-side (DB/cache). Server sends a cookie: Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123. Browser sends the cookie on every request: Cookie: sessionid=abc123. Server looks up the session: session_id → user. Where data lives: Location Stores Browser session_id (cookie only) Server Full user session data Pros: Very secure (server controls state); easy in Django via request.user; built-in support. Cons: Needs server memory for sessions; harder to scale across multiple servers; not ideal for APIs/mobile apps.

In settings.py: MIDDLEWARE = [ ..., 'django.contrib.sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware', 'django.contrib.auth.middleware.AuthenticationMiddleware', ] Authentication is not done by SessionMiddleware alone — it's a combination of two middlewares: Middleware Job SessionMiddleware Reads the sessionid cookie, loads session data from server, attaches request.session AuthenticationMiddleware Reads request.session, gets the user_id, fetches the user, attaches request.user Flow: Browser sends cookie: sessionid=abc123 ↓ SessionMiddleware → request.session = session_data ↓ AuthenticationMiddleware → request.user = User object Session data is just a key-value store in server,tied to a specific user's browsing session.it is like _auth_user_id: 5, _auth_user_hash: "...",request.session["theme"] = "dark" etc. You only need to write custom code when doing custom authentication (JWT, tokens), WebSocket auth, or custom user logic. Django Channels equivalent: WebSocket connections in Django Channels don't have a request object — they have a scope instead. So Django's normal HTTP-only middlewares (SessionMiddleware, AuthenticationMiddleware) can't be used directly — they're written specifically to work with request, not scope. Django Channels provides AuthMiddlewareStack — think of it as: "the WebSocket-compatible version of SessionMiddleware + AuthenticationMiddleware, bundled together as one thing."
documents.s: How Django Implements This by Default

documents.t: A session is data stored on the server to remember a user between requests (e.g., request.session["user_id"] = 5), stored in a database or cache (like Redis). A cookie is a small piece of data — typically just the session_id — stored in the browser. Key rule: Cookie stores only the session_id. Session (server-side) stores the actual user data. Cookie ≠ Session. The cookie is just a key; the session is the data. Full flow: User logs in (username + password → server). Server creates a session: {"abc123": {"user_id": 5}}. Server sends Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123 — stored in the browser. On the next request, the browser automatically sends Cookie: sessionid=abc123 (sent as an HTTP request header, e.g., Cookie: sessionid=abc123; theme=dark). Server looks up abc123 → user and sets request.user. In Django, request.session is a Python object (interface) used to read/write session data — example: request.session["name"] = "John" 1. You write this line in your view ↓ 2. request.session (the interface object) receives this write ↓ 3. Internally, it marks "name" → "John" as a change to be saved ↓ 4. At the end of the request, Django's SessionMiddleware takes request.session's data and SAVES it into the real storage (DB row / Redis entry) tied to that session_id
This portion covers authentication techniques
Ensuring secure access and identity management.

Full form: JSON Web Token A compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties. It allows for stateless authentication, meaning the server doesn't need to store session data in a database to verify the user. NOTE: You can't truly do stateless. You must have a blacklist in memory for various reasons, like logged out users, compromised tokens, etc.

A stateful, server-side storage mechanism where user data is kept in memory or a database, identified by a unique ID. It offers maximum security and control; the server can instantly revoke access by deleting the session from its storage, which is harder to do with stateless JWTs.

Full form: Single Sign-On A session and user authentication service that permits a user to use one set of login credentials to access multiple applications. It creates a seamless User Experience (UX) and reduces "password fatigue" while centralizing security management for an organization. OAuth 2.0 is an industry-standard framework for authorization, allowing apps to securely access user data (on platforms like Google or Facebook) without needing user credentials. While not an authentication protocol itself, it authorizes actions via access tokens. It is often combined with OpenID Connect for authentication.

Full form: Open Authorization 2.0 An industry-standard protocol for delegated authorization, allowing apps to access data on behalf of a user without seeing their password (e.g., "Login with Google"). It limits exposure; a third-party app only gets a scoped token to do specific tasks rather than having full access to your account.

Full form: OpenID Connect An identity layer built on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol that allows clients to verify the identity of the end-user. It provides a standardized way to handle "Login"; while OAuth 2.0 is about delegating access (authorization), OIDC is about proving identity (authentication).
How do we save / handle the token or session id on the client side?

A method where the server uses a browser's cookie storage to automatically send and receive authentication data (either a Session ID or a JWT) with every request. It is effortless for the developer because the browser handles sending the cookie automatically, and it can be made highly secure using the HttpOnly flag to prevent XSS attacks.

A method where the client manually attaches a token (usually a JWT) to the Authorization header of an HTTP request (e.g., Authorization: Bearer xxx). It is built for mobile apps and cross-domain APIs where browsers might block cookies or where you aren't using a browser at all. The token may be saved in different places depending on the application or device. KeyChain (iOS), Keystore (Android), Local storage (browser) or session storage (browser)
This section covers the mechanics of how you keep a user logged in and how you kick them out when necessary.

A short-lived credential (often a JWT) used by the client to access protected resources. It allows for fast, stateless verification without hitting the database for every single API request. It's often paired with a refresh token

A long-lived credential used solely to obtain a new access token (often a JWT) once the current one expires. It balances security and UX by allowing users to stay logged in for days without keeping a highly sensitive access token active that entire time.

A security pattern where every time a refresh token is used, it is invalidated and replaced with a brand-new one. It detects malicious reuse; if an attacker steals a token and uses it, the real user’s subsequent attempt will fail, alerting the system to revoke the entire session.

Full form: Session Identifier A unique string stored in a cookie that points to a specific data record on the server (in RAM or DB). It provides instant revocation control; if a user’s account is compromised, the admin can delete the session server-side to log them out immediately.
Full form: Time To Live A hard-coded timestamp within a token (the exp claim) that defines when it becomes invalid. It limits the "window of opportunity" for an attacker if a token is intercepted.

The process of maintaining a list of "invalid" JWTs (usually in Redis) that haven't expired yet but should no longer be accepted. It solves the main weakness of stateless JWTs, allowing you to log a user out or ban them before their token naturally expires.

A technique where the expiration time of a session is extended every time the user makes a new request. It keeps active users logged in indefinitely while automatically cleaning up and logging out inactive users after a period of idleness.
This portion covers security related questions & techniques

Full form: Role-Based Access Control An approach to restricting system access to authorized users based on their assigned roles (e.g., Admin, Editor, Viewer). It simplifies permission management; instead of managing permissions for 1,000 users, you manage them for 5 roles and simply assign users to those roles.

Full form: Attribute-Based Access Control A logical access control methodology where access rights are granted through the use of policies which combine attributes (user, resource, and environment). It provides fine-grained control; for example, you can create a rule saying "Only Managers (User Attribute) can view Financial Reports (Resource Attribute) if they are accessing from the office IP (Environment Attribute)."
RBAC is better for smaller, standard applications where roles are clearly defined. ABAC is better for complex, enterprise-scale systems that require high security and context-aware permissions.
How we protect the infrastructure from abuse and high-volume traffic.

A technique to control the number of requests a user or IP can make to a service within a specific timeframe. It prevents DoS/DDoS attacks and ensures that a single "noisy neighbor" (a user making too many requests) doesn't crash the server for everyone else. Rate Limiting algorithms Token Bucket: Users are given a "bucket" of tokens. Every request uses one. Tokens refill at a steady rate. (Allows for "bursts" of traffic). Leaky Bucket: Requests enter a bucket and "leak" out to be processed at a constant, fixed rate. (Ensures a very steady, predictable flow; no bursts allowed). Fixed Window: Limits requests per calendar minute (e.g., 100 requests between 10:00 and 10:01). (Easy to implement but can be "gamed" at the minute boundaries). Sliding Window: A more accurate version of Fixed Window that looks at the exact previous 60 seconds from the current timestamp.

The process of gradually slowing down a user’s responses once they near their limit, rather than hard-blocking them. It provides a better user experience (UX) by allowing a user to finish their task at a slower speed instead of receiving an immediate "429 Too Many Requests" error.

Full form: Web Application Firewall A specialized firewall that monitors, filters, and blocks HTTP traffic to and from a web application. It provides edge-level protection against common attacks like SQL Injection and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) before the traffic even touches your internal network.

A configuration that rejects any incoming request if the body (JSON/Image) exceeds a pre-defined size (e.g., 5MB). It prevents memory exhaustion attacks; without this, an attacker could send a 1GB JSON file that crashes your server’s memory while it tries to parse it.

A network security mechanism that allows (whitelists) or blocks (blacklists) traffic based on the sender's IP address. It secures internal services; for example, a production database should only allow connections from the specific IP addresses of your application servers.

Fail-Closed (Best for Auth): If the auth service is down, the system denies all access. Better to be down than compromised. Fail-Open (Best for UX): If a non-critical feature (like a recommendation engine) fails, the user can still use the core app.

A security strategy that uses multiple layers of defense so that if one layer fails, others are in place to stop the attack. It assumes that no single security measure is perfect; by layering WAF, API Gateways, and Hashing, you make it exponentially harder for an attacker to reach the core data.

Full form: Principle of Least Privilege The practice of giving a user, process, or system only the minimum level of access necessary to perform its job. It minimizes the "Blast Radius"; if a specific service is compromised, the attacker can't move laterally to the rest of the system because that service had restricted permissions.

A security model based on the principle of "Never Trust, Always Verify," even for users or devices already inside the corporate network. It prevents attackers from moving freely inside a network once they get past the initial firewall, which is how most major data breaches escalate.

Encrypting data while it is stored on a physical disk (e.g., in a database or cloud storage). It protects against physical theft or unauthorized access to the server's hard drives or database backups.

Encrypting data as it moves across a network between the client and the server (e.g., via HTTPS/TLS). It prevents "Man-in-the-Middle" (MITM) attacks, ensuring that attackers cannot sniff or alter sensitive data as it travels through the internet.

While often discussed in Performance, Idempotency is a security/reliability feature. Concept: Ensuring that making the same request multiple times (e.g., "Pay $10") has the same effect as making it once. It prevents double-charges or duplicate data entries if a user clicks a button twice or if a network retry happens automatically.

The Problem: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) allows an attacker to run JavaScript in your user's browser. Since Local Storage is accessible via JS, a simple localStorage.getItem() can steal the token. The Mitigation Content Security Policy (CSP): Restrict which scripts can run and where they can send data. Input Sanitization: Never trust user input; encode it to prevent script injection in browsers. The "Gold Standard": Don't store sensitive tokens in Local Storage. Keep the Access Token (JWT) in memory (JS variable) and the Refresh Token in an HttpOnly cookie.
documents.s: Q: If I store my JWT in Local Storage, how do I prevent XSS from stealing it?

documents.t: The Problem: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) tricks a logged-in user's browser into sending an unwanted request (like "transfer money") because the browser automatically attaches cookies to every request to that domain. Anti-CSRF Tokens: Include a unique, hidden token in every state-changing request (POST/PUT) that the server validates. SameSite Cookie Attribute: Set your cookie to SameSite=Strict or Lax so the browser won't send it during cross-site requests.
documents.s: Q: If I use Cookies to store my Session ID, how do I prevent CSRF?

documents.t: HttpOnly: Prevents JavaScript from accessing the cookie (mitigates XSS). Secure: Ensures the cookie is only sent over encrypted HTTPS connections. SameSite: Controls whether the cookie is sent with cross-site requests (mitigates CSRF).
documents.s: Q: What are the essential Cookie security flags?

documents.t: It should be multi-layered. At the Edge (WAF/CDN): To block massive DDoS attacks. At the API Gateway: To manage per-user/per-key quotas. At the Service Level: To protect specific "expensive" database queries or third-party API calls.
documents.s: Q: Where should you implement Rate Limiting?

documents.t: The Answer: Choose ABAC when access depends on context—like time of day, location, or specific resource ownership—rather than just a job title. Example: Think of RBAC as a VIP list at a club (Are you on the list? Come in). Think of ABAC as a bouncer checking your ID, your sobriety, and if you’re wearing the right shoes (context matters). Example: A healthcare app where a "Doctor" (Role) can only view a "Patient Record" (Resource) if they are the "Assigned Physician" (Attribute).
documents.s: Q: When would you choose ABAC over RBAC in a real-world project?

documents.t: The Answer: Bursts. Token Bucket allows for a sudden burst of requests as long as there are tokens in the bucket. Leaky Bucket enforces a strict, uniform output rate regardless of the incoming pressure. Why it matters: Use Token Bucket for APIs where users might occasionally need to load a lot of data at once; use Leaky Bucket for background processing tasks where you want to ensure your database never sees a spike.
documents.s: Q: What is the biggest practical difference between the Token Bucket and Leaky Bucket algorithms?

documents.t: The Answer: Rate Limiting is a "brick wall" (429 error); Throttling is "molasses." It degrades the user experience gracefully by slowing down the response time rather than breaking the integration entirely. Why it matters: This prevents a client’s automated retry logic from slamming your server the microsecond the rate-limit window resets (preventing the "thundering herd" problem).
documents.s: Q: Why is Throttling considered "kinder" than simple Rate Limiting?

documents.t: The Answer: No. This is called Defense in Depth. A WAF catches broad, known patterns (like common SQL injection strings) at the edge. The Peer Perspective: Your application still needs to validate business logic that a WAF can’t see—like ensuring a user isn't trying to withdraw $1,000 from an account with a $50 balance.
documents.s: Q: Does a WAF (Web Application Firewall) make my application-level validation redundant?

documents.t: The Answer: It prevents Memory Exhaustion DoS attacks. An attacker could send a massive, multi-gigabyte JSON "bomb" that forces your server to use all its RAM during parsing, crashing the service for everyone. NOTE: Mention that this should be configured at the Nginx/Load Balancer level so the "fat" request never even reaches your app code.
documents.s: Q: Why is Payload Size Limiting considered a "security" feature and not just a performance one?

documents.t: The Answer: For the public side? No, because users have dynamic IPs. But for internal communication (e.g., your Admin Panel only accepting traffic from your office VPN), it is an incredibly effective "lock" that stops random internet scans.
documents.s: Q: Is IP Whitelisting a valid security strategy for a modern, public-facing Web App?

documents.t: The Problem: Hardcoding API keys or DB passwords in your source code is a major security risk. The Solution: Use Environment Variables or dedicated Secret Managers (like AWS Secrets Manager). The Peer Insight: Secrets should be encrypted and injected into the application at runtime, never checked into Git.
documents.s: Q: How do you handle "Secrets Management" in a production environment?

documents.t: Full form: HTTP Strict Transport Security A security header that tells the browser to only communicate with the server via HTTPS, even if the user types http://. It prevents SSL Stripping attacks, where an attacker tries to downgrade your connection to unencrypted HTTP to steal your cookies.
documents.s: Q: What is HSTS and why should we care?
